title: Multi-tenant burst allocator with soft eviction playbook
preview:
Problem: High-frequency burst traffic from a small set of tenants occasionally exhausts local quota tokens and triggers hard rejects or cross-region failovers. When the central limiter or its lease-store experiences transient outages, we currently fall back to a conservative deny-all behavior that leads to large SLO violations for affected customers. Goal: implement a resilient multi-tenant burst allocator that gracefully degrades under limiter outages and sustained overloads: - allow short, proportionally-weighted burst absorption using a token escrow backed by per-tenant standing reservations - introduce a soft-eviction policy to preempt low-priority work rather than hard reject high-priority requests - integrate with circuit-breaker signals so allocator behavior automatically widens or tightens depending on downstream health - provide deterministic admission behavior during global limiter outages by supporting a read-only fallback allocator with configurable safety caps - surface telemetry and alerts tied to SLOs and fairness regressions

Proposed solution summary: build an in-path Allocator service implemented as a stateless admission layer that consults a fast local lease cache and an eventual central bank for long-term accounting. Key mechanisms: 1) Escrow buffer: small, per-tenant short-term credits (milliseconds-to-seconds) that absorb microbursts. Credits decay and are replenished from the central bank at a bounded rate. 2) Weighted soft-eviction: when capacity is exhausted, the allocator preempts lower-priority requests (background embeddings, low-QoS retries) and returns a 202-driven defer response with retry hints instead of a 429 hard reject. 3) Emergency reserve and preemption order: reserve a configurable emergency pool for tenants tagged as SLA-critical; preemption order derived from tenant weight, recent usage, and emergency flag. 4) Circuit integration: allocator subscribes to regional circuit-breaker state; when downstream latency or error budget breaches occur, allocator shifts to aggressive preemption and tighter caps. 5) Read-only outage fallback: if central bank is unavailable, allocator uses a deterministic, conservative reservation algorithm (last-known quotas with decay) to avoid wide open fail-open. 6) Observability: fine-grained telemetry for per-tenant unfairness score, escrow consumption, preemption events, and fallback activation. 7) Chaos & sim-first rollout: validate in a simulator and run progressive chaos experiments before cluster rollout.

title: Add integration tests: sensitive actions emit v1 audit events (hosted + private fixtures)
preview:
Context
We are standardizing audit logging across Hosted/Dedicated/Private and gating launch on “100% coverage for sensitive actions.” Unit tests in individual services are not sufficient to prevent regressions (new endpoints / UI flows missing audit emission, missing required fields, inconsistent actor attribution). This ticket adds end-to-end tests that perform representative sensitive actions and assert that a correctly-shaped schema v1 audit event is produced.

Goals
- Catch missing audit events for sensitive actions during CI before merge.
- Validate schema v1 shape + required fields via the shared validator library.
- Validate actor/target semantics and correlation fields (request_id/trace_id/admin_action_id) for investigation workflows.
- Ensure parity: the same action in Hosted fixture and Private fixture yields an equivalent event_type + required fields.

Non-goals
- Building a customer-facing audit log UI.
- Full coverage of every UI click path (we will target canonical backend actions; UI flows are covered via Console backend tests where feasible).
- Validating delivery guarantees end-to-end to external SIEMs (covered by pipeline SLO/alerting and exporter tests). This suite focuses on event production + correctness.

Test strategy (high level)
We implement a small “audit test harness” that can:
1) Execute a sensitive action via the public/admin API surface used by customers (or Console backend endpoint where that is the canonical producer).
2) Capture produced audit events from a deterministic sink in test mode.
3) Assert:
   - event_type is registered / expected
   - schema_version is present and matches v1.x
   - JSON payload validates against schema v1 (using the shared schema library)
   - required fields are present and non-empty (tenant/org/project where applicable)
   - actor is correct (user vs service vs system; impersonation present only when used)
   - no secret/PII leaks (ensure redaction placeholders rather than raw values)
   - correlation IDs align (request_id in API response header matches audit event request_id; trace_id present when tracing enabled)

Deterministic audit sink
To avoid flakiness from async pipelines, the services will support a test-only sink mode:
- Hosted fixture: start control-plane + gateway in “AUDIT_SINK=http” mode, pointing to an in-process collector (test container) that buffers events by (event_id, request_id).
- Private fixture: same collector runs inside the docker-compose/kind stack; configure via installer values or env overrides.

Notes:
- We will not depend on the exporter, Kafka-like queues, or production ingestion during these tests. We only need the event producer to emit successfully.
- We keep strict time bounds: wait up to 5s for the expected event by request_id (polling collector endpoint).

Test matrix (initial set)
The matrix is intentionally limited to the highest-risk, highest-frequency sensitive actions that have historically regressed or are customer-reviewed in security questionnaires.

A) RBAC / access
1. Create role (custom role)
   - Action: POST /rbac/roles
   - Expect: rbac.role.create
2. Update role permissions
   - Action: PATCH /rbac/roles/{id}
   - Expect: rbac.role.update (includes diff_summary)
3. Delete role
   - Action: DELETE /rbac/roles/{id}
   - Expect: rbac.role.delete

B) Credential lifecycle
4. Create API key
   - Action: POST /api-keys
   - Expect: credential.api_key.create
   - Assert: key material is NOT present; only key_id / key_prefix allowed per redaction rules
5. Rotate API key
   - Action: POST /api-keys/{id}/rotate
   - Expect: credential.api_key.rotate
6. Revoke API key
   - Action: POST /api-keys/{id}/revoke
   - Expect: credential.api_key.revoke

C) Policy / routing / rollouts
7. Update routing policy (fallback/allowlist)
   - Action: PUT /policy/routing
   - Expect: policy.routing.update
   - Assert: diff_summary is bounded (no full policy blob; caps enforced)

D) Data retention / residency
8. Update retention window
   - Action: PUT /settings/retention
   - Expect: data.retention.update (previous/new values summarized)
9. Update residency region
   - Action: PUT /settings/residency
   - Expect: data.residency.update

E) Audit export configuration (because customers ask for it explicitly)
10. Configure export destination
   - Action: PUT /audit/export
   - Expect: audit.export.update
   - Assert: destination URLs are normalized and secrets are redacted (no raw tokens)

Private-only follow-ups (tracked separately)
- Installer upgrade/rollback events are emitted from the installer repo and require a heavier harness. We will add a minimal smoke test in the installer pipeline; not part of this suite (see ENG-4826).

Assertions (schema + semantics)
For each captured event:
- Validate against schema v1 via shared validator (see PR-21984).
- Required fields we assert (non-exhaustive):
  - schema_version
  - event_id (stable id for dedupe)
  - event_type
  - occurred_at
  - tenant_id (and project_id if scoped)
  - actor_type + actor_id (or actor_principal)
  - source_ip (when action is user-initiated via gateway; may be absent for system/async)
  - request_id (always for HTTP actions)
  - user_agent (for gateway-originated console actions; optional otherwise)
  - target_resource_type + target_resource_id for CRUD actions
- Negative assertions:
  - No secrets: api keys, bearer tokens, private keys, SSO assertions
  - No high-cardinality blobs: full policy JSON, full request payloads, arbitrary user-provided strings without allow-list

CI integration / gating
- Hosted: run in main integration test workflow (fast subset) on every PR touching control-plane/gateway/console-backend audit producers.
- Private: run in nightly + “required for merge” only for changes under /private/ and /installer/ related config surfaces (to keep CI time bounded). If we can keep private fixture <15 min, we can make it required for merge across all audit touches.
- Gating rule: new sensitive action coverage (as defined in PM-938 and taxonomy) must add/extend an E2E test or provide an approved exception.

Implementation plan
1) Add audit-test-collector
- Simple HTTP server with:
  - POST /collect (audit sink)
  - GET /events?request_id=…
  - GET /health
- Stores events in memory with TTL and exposes debug endpoints guarded by test env.

2) Update fixtures
- Hosted docker-compose: configure gateway/control-plane to emit to collector.
- Private fixture: pass AUDIT_SINK_URL via values/env into the stack.

3) Write tests
- Use existing e2e harness (supertest/go test harness depending on suite) to:
  - perform action
  - capture request_id from response headers
  - poll collector until event is present
  - run schema validation + semantic assertions

4) Stabilize
- Add retry/poll with bounded timeout.
- Ensure events are filtered by tenant_id + request_id to avoid cross-test bleed.
- Run with parallelism disabled initially; revisit when stable.

Acceptance criteria
- Tests exist for items 1–10 above and run in CI at least for Hosted.
- Tests validate schema v1 using the shared validator; no hand-rolled field checking only.
- Tests assert at minimum: correct event_type, schema_version, event_id, occurred_at, tenant_id, actor attribution, request_id.
- Private fixture executes at least one representative action from each category (RBAC, credential, policy, retention/residency, export config) and confirms parity.
- Documented in repo: how to run locally, how to add a new sensitive-action integration test.

Risks / open questions
- Source IP availability in Private (behind internal ingress) may differ; we should assert presence only when expected by producer context.
- Correlation propagation: some actions might be async; those should use admin_action_id instead of request_id. For this ticket we focus on synchronous HTTP actions.
- Flakiness from shared environments: mitigate by using unique tenant per test run.

Links
- PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-22058-add-integration-tests-sensitive-actions-produce-audit-events.json
- Related: ENG-4821 (schema), ENG-4822 (taxonomy registry), ENG-4830 (correlation IDs), ENG-4831 (CI required fields lint)

Owner notes
Leila to coordinate with Logan Wright (coverage dashboard expectations) and Sean Gallagher (CI/runtime reliability). If we need to relax required-for-merge for Private due to runtime, we should explicitly document the rationale and add a follow-up to reduce fixture time.

title: Policy engine: add auto-rollback config schema, validation, and migration for rollout policies
preview:
Context
We need to extend the control plane policy engine schema to support SLO-driven auto-actions during rollouts (pause and rollback). This ticket covers:
1) schema changes (policy/config) that describe thresholds/windows/guardrails
2) validation (static + cross-field) with safe bounds to prevent flapping/unsafe configs
3) migration plan for existing rollout policies so default behavior is unchanged (auto-rollback disabled unless explicitly enabled)

Goals
- Define canonical policy shape for auto-actions on SLO breach, aligned with PRD + design spec and ADR-0127.
- Ensure misconfigured policies fail fast with actionable error messages in Console/API.
- Provide an explicit migration plan for existing stored rollout policies, including versioning/defaulting.

Non-goals
- Controller-side evaluation logic (ENG-4821 / ENG-4861).
- Audit event schema and event emission (ADR-0128, ENG-4831 / PR-28435).

Proposed schema (high level)
Add `rollout.auto_actions` section (name TBD; PM/Console copy uses “Auto-rollback”). Initial fields:
- enabled: bool (default false)
- action_on_breach: enum { "pause", "rollback", "shift_to_baseline" } (MVP supports pause+rollback; A/B shift may be limited to routes using traffic-split)
- evaluation:
  - fast_window: duration (e.g., "5m")
  - slow_window: duration (e.g., "30m")
  - burn_rate_threshold_fast: float
  - burn_rate_threshold_slow: float
  - min_requests_fast: int
  - min_requests_slow: int
  - debounce:
    - holdoff_after_step_change: duration (e.g., "3m")
    - min_breach_duration: duration (e.g., "2m")
- signals:
  - latency_p95_enabled: bool
  - latency_p99_enabled: bool
  - error_rate_enabled: bool
  - saturation_enabled: bool (optional / only for Dedicated)
  - NOTE: actual SLO monitor IDs are resolved by rollout scope (route/model/region/tier) by controller/monitor-adapter; policy only defines which signals are eligible + thresholds/burn-rate criteria
- suppression:
  - allow_during_incident: bool (default false) or inverse: suppress_during_incident: bool (default true)
  - manual_suppression_max_duration: duration (guardrail; e.g., "24h")
  - auto_suppress_after_n_actions: int (optional; guardrail to prevent loops)
- bounds/guardrails (enforced in validation): see below

Validation requirements
Static validation (schema-level)
- Durations parseable and within bounds.
- Enums restricted; unknown fields rejected (strict).
- Numeric fields non-negative; floats finite.

Cross-field validation (business rules)
- If enabled=true, action_on_breach must be set.
- fast_window < slow_window.
- burn_rate_threshold_fast >= burn_rate_threshold_slow (fast burn should be more sensitive).
- min_requests_* must be >= minimum safe floor (avoid 1-2 request flukes).
- holdoff_after_step_change must be >= telemetry lag floor and <= max to avoid masking real regressions.
- If action_on_breach=rollback, require rollback-capable rollout types (canary/staged) OR ensure controller will downgrade to pause with a validation warning surfaced (we need a decision: fail vs degrade). Proposed: fail validation for unsupported rollout type in policy; keep semantics simple.

Safe bounds (initial proposal; confirm with SRE/PM)
- fast_window: [1m, 10m], default 5m
- slow_window: [10m, 2h], default 30m
- burn_rate_threshold_fast: [1.0, 50.0], default 14
- burn_rate_threshold_slow: [1.0, 20.0], default 6
- min_requests_fast: [100, 100000], default 500 (tier-dependent)
- min_requests_slow: [500, 500000], default 2000 (tier-dependent)
- holdoff_after_step_change: [1m, 15m], default 3m
- min_breach_duration: [0m, fast_window], default 2m
- manual_suppression_max_duration: [15m, 7d], default 24h

Defaults / templates
PM-618 defines per-tier defaults (self-serve vs dedicated vs private). Implementation detail:
- Policy engine should support server-side defaults resolved by `customer_tier` and environment OR accept fully specified policy from Console templates.
- Proposed approach for MVP: Console writes fully specified config (template expansion happens in Console), but policy engine also enforces safe bounds regardless. (We can add server-managed templates later.)

Migration plan
Current state: rollout policies exist in policy-store without any auto-action fields.
Plan:
1) Introduce schema version bump: `policy_version` (or reuse existing `schema_version` field) with forward/backward compatibility.
2) When reading older policies:
   - default `auto_actions.enabled=false`
   - do not implicitly enable based on any existing SLO configs
3) When writing via API:
   - accept both old and new payloads for one release window (2-4 weeks), but always persist in latest normalized form
4) Data migration:
   - background job to rewrite existing policies into normalized latest schema with `enabled=false` (optional but recommended to reduce mixed versions)
   - include dry-run mode and metrics: migrated_count, failed_count, validation_failures
5) Rollback strategy:
   - because new fields are additive and default disabled, rollback consists of ignoring new fields (server can keep reading old version) but must avoid rejecting persisted policies. Keep read-path tolerant.

API / storage impacts
- Policy engine API: add new fields to rollout policy endpoints; update OpenAPI/SDK models.
- Config service: ensure caching keys include schema version; no breaking change to cache structure.
- RBAC: not in scope here (admin override is separate), but ensure policy writing follows existing permissions. Console gating is separate.

Implementation plan
- Add typed struct/model + JSON schema (or equivalent validation layer) for `auto_actions`.
- Implement validation errors with field paths (e.g., `rollout.auto_actions.evaluation.fast_window`).
- Add unit tests:
  - happy paths per tier defaults
  - boundary tests for each guardrail
  - backward compatibility tests with old policies
- Add migration utilities:
  - normalize(old_policy) -> new_policy
  - CLI/job scaffolding for backfill
- Instrumentation:
  - policy_write_validation_failures_total{reason}
  - policy_migration_* metrics if we run the job

Acceptance criteria
- New schema fields accepted + persisted; old payloads still accepted during compatibility window.
- Validation rejects unsafe configs with actionable messages; guardrail limits match PM/SRE agreed ranges.
- Read-path is tolerant across versions; no production policy load failures during rollout.
- Migration plan written up in the ticket and reviewed by Console/rollout-controller owners.

Open questions
- Do we fail validation or degrade action when rollout type doesn’t support rollback? (proposal: fail; keep config explicit)
- Where do tier defaults live for MVP: Console template expansion vs policy-engine tier-aware defaults?
- How do we express “which SLOs” precisely? Keep boolean flags vs explicit list of signal kinds. (proposal: explicit list/enum to avoid a proliferation of booleans)

Risks
- Misaligned defaults vs SRE recommendation leads to noise or missed rollbacks.
- Too-strict schema breaks existing Console writes; coordinate release window.
- Cardinality/complexity: avoid encoding monitor IDs directly in policy to prevent churn and brittle configs.

Reviewers
- Jordan Lee (policy evaluation)
- Jada Williams (rollout controller)
- Sean Gallagher (SRE semantics)
- Marissa Cole / Paula Mendes (product sign-off on user-visible semantics)

Notes
- Keep the config names stable; Console will likely mirror them in the policy editor UI (DES-951). Changes after Console ships are expensive.
- Align debounce/holdoff semantics with ADR-0127 so evaluation doesn’t flap on step boundaries.

    - holdoff_after_step_change: duration (e.g., "3m")
    - min_breach_duration: duration (e.g., "2m")
- signals:
  - latency_p95_enabled: bool
  - latency_p99_enabled: bool
  - error_rate_enabled: bool
  - saturation_enabled: bool (optional / only for Dedicated)
  - NOTE: actual SLO monitor IDs are resolved by rollout scope (route/model/region/tier) by controller/monitor-adapter; policy only defines which signals are eligible + thresholds/burn-rate criteria
- suppression:
  - allow_during_incident: bool (default false) or inverse: suppress_during_incident: bool (default true)
  - manual_suppression_max_duration: duration (guardrail; e.g., "24h")
  - auto_suppress_after_n_actions: int (optional; guardrail to prevent loops)
- bounds/guardrails (enforced in validation): see below

Validation requirements
Static validation (schema-level)
- Durations parseable and within bounds.
- Enums restricted; unknown fields rejected (strict).
- Numeric fields non-negative; floats finite.

Cross-field validation (business rules)
- If enabled=true, action_on_breach must be set.
- fast_window < slow_window.
- burn_rate_threshold_fast >= burn_rate_threshold_slow (fast burn should be more sensitive).
- min_requests_* must be >= minimum safe floor (avoid 1-2 request flukes).
- holdoff_after_step_change must be >= telemetry lag floor and <= max to avoid masking real regressions.
- If action_on_breach=rollback, require rollback-capable rollout types (canary/staged) OR ensure controller will downgrade to pause with a validation warning surfaced (we need a decision: fail vs degrade). Proposed: fail validation for unsupported rollout type in policy; keep semantics simple.

Safe bounds (initial proposal; confirm with SRE/PM)
- fast_window: [1m, 10m], default 5m
- slow_window: [10m, 2h], default 30m
- burn_rate_threshold_fast: [1.0, 50.0], default 14
- burn_rate_threshold_slow: [1.0, 20.0], default 6
- min_requests_fast: [100, 100000], default 500 (tier-dependent)
- min_requests_slow: [500, 500000], default 2000 (tier-dependent)
- holdoff_after_step_change: [1m, 15m], default 3m
- min_breach_duration: [0m, fast_window], default 2m
- manual_suppression_max_duration: [15m, 7d], default 24h

Defaults / templates
PM-618 defines per-tier defaults (self-serve vs dedicated vs private). Implementation detail:
- Policy engine should support server-side defaults resolved by `customer_tier` and environment OR accept fully specified policy from Console templates.
- Proposed approach for MVP: Console writes fully specified config (template expansion happens in Console), but policy engine also enforces safe bounds regardless. (We can add server-managed templates later.)

Migration plan
Current state: rollout policies exist in policy-store without any auto-action fields.
Plan:
1) Introduce schema version bump: `policy_version` (or reuse existing `schema_version` field) with forward/backward compatibility.
2) When reading older policies:
   - default `auto_actions.enabled=false`
   - do not implicitly enable based on any existing SLO configs
3) When writing via API:
   - accept both old and new payloads for one release window (2-4 weeks), but always persist in latest normalized form
4) Data migration:
   - background job to rewrite existing policies into normalized latest schema with `enabled=false` (optional but recommended to reduce mixed versions)
   - include dry-run mode and metrics: migrated_count, failed_count, validation_failures
5) Rollback strategy:
   - because new fields are additive and default disabled, rollback consists of ignoring new fields (server can keep reading old version) but must avoid rejecting persisted policies. Keep read-path tolerant.

API / storage impacts
- Policy engine API: add new fields to rollout policy endpoints; update OpenAPI/SDK models.
- Config service: ensure caching keys include schema version; no breaking change to cache structure.
- RBAC: not in scope here (admin override is separate), but ensure policy writing follows existing permissions. Console gating is separate.

Implementation plan
- Add typed struct/model + JSON schema (or equivalent validation layer) for `auto_actions`.
- Implement validation errors with field paths (e.g., `rollout.auto_actions.evaluation.fast_window`).
- Add unit tests:
  - happy paths per tier defaults
  - boundary tests for each guardrail
  - backward compatibility tests with old policies
- Add migration utilities:
  - normalize(old_policy) -> new_policy
  - CLI/job scaffolding for backfill
- Instrumentation:
  - policy_write_validation_failures_total{reason}
  - policy_migration_* metrics if we run the job

Acceptance criteria
- New schema fields accepted + persisted; old payloads still accepted during compatibility window.
- Validation rejects unsafe configs with actionable messages; guardrail limits match PM/SRE agreed ranges.
- Read-path is tolerant across versions; no production policy load failures during rollout.
- Migration plan written up in the ticket and reviewed by Console/rollout-controller owners.

Open questions
- Do we fail validation or degrade action when rollout type doesn’t support rollback? (proposal: fail; keep config explicit)
- Where do tier defaults live for MVP: Console template expansion vs policy-engine tier-aware defaults?
- How do we express “which SLOs” precisely? Keep boolean flags vs explicit list of signal kinds. (proposal: explicit list/enum to avoid a proliferation of booleans)

Risks
- Misaligned defaults vs SRE recommendation leads to noise or missed rollbacks.
- Too-strict schema breaks existing Console writes; coordinate release window.
- Cardinality/complexity: avoid encoding monitor IDs directly in policy to prevent churn and brittle configs.

Reviewers
- Jordan Lee (policy evaluation)
- Jada Williams (rollout controller)
- Sean Gallagher (SRE semantics)
- Marissa Cole / Paula Mendes (product sign-off on user-visible semantics)

Notes
- Keep the config names stable; Console will likely mirror them in the policy editor UI (DES-951). Changes after Console ships are expensive.
- Align debounce/holdoff semantics with ADR-0127 so evaluation doesn’t flap on step boundaries.

title: Kernel auto-selection v2: per-request telemetry fields + sampling policy
preview:
## Goal
Add per-request telemetry for kernel auto-selection (v2) with a sampling policy that keeps overhead + metric cardinality bounded, while enabling:
- rollout monitoring (selection distribution + perf deltas)
- fast triage for tail-latency spikes / suspected mis-selections
- deterministic replay/debug (record inputs to the decision, not the prompt)

This ticket covers:
1) finalizing the telemetry schema (fields + naming)
2) implementing emission in runtime (events + counters/histograms)
3) sampling policy + controls (defaults, canary overrides, and “escalation” behavior)
4) dashboards/queries compatibility constraints (cardinality limits)

Non-goals:
- storing prompts or token content (PII/security)
- end-to-end dashboards (tracked elsewhere; this ticket provides the data contract)

## Context
Kernel auto-selection v2 makes a decision keyed on model family/architecture, seq-length regime (prefill/decode), and GPU SKU. We need enough signal per request to answer:
- what kernel was chosen and why?
- what other candidates were considered?
- what inputs/features were used (model family, seq bucket, GPU SKU)?
- did we hit a fallback/denylist?

Constraints:
- must not meaningfully impact p95/p99 latency
- must avoid high-cardinality explosion in metrics
- must be safe for Dedicated/Private environments (sampling defaults, config knobs, and “break glass” debug mode)

## Proposed telemetry contract
We emit two categories:
A) **Lightweight metrics** (bounded labels) for aggregate monitoring.
B) **Structured per-request event** (sampled) for debugging/forensics.

### A) Metrics (always on)
**Counters**
- `runtime.kernel_selector.decisions_total{selector_version, gate_state, model_family, gpu_sku, seq_bucket, chosen_kernel, fallback_reason}`
  - Label constraints:
    - `model_family`: small fixed set (llama/mistral/qwen/gemma/other)
    - `gpu_sku`: normalized family+mem tier (e.g., a100-80g, h100-80g, l40s-48g, other)
    - `chosen_kernel`: short ID from registry (bounded)
    - `fallback_reason`: enum (none, missing_kernel, denylist_hit, unstable_driver, determinism_guardrail, compat_mismatch, error)

**Histograms**
- `runtime.kernel_selector.eval_latency_us{selector_version}`
- `runtime.kernel_selector.candidate_count{selector_version, model_family}`

**Gauges (optional / if already supported)**
- `runtime.kernel_selector.allowlist_version` (build/config version string is logged elsewhere; gauge only if cheap)

### B) Sampled per-request event: `kernel_selection_decision`
Event is emitted as a structured log / trace event (preferred: OTEL span event on request span). Fields:
- **Identity / gating**
  - `selector_version` (v1/v2)
  - `gate_state` (disabled, enabled, shadow, forced)
  - `rollout_rule_id` (if applicable; stable short ID)
  - `allowlist_hit` (true/false)
  - `denylist_hit` (true/false)

- **Model & hardware (no prompt content)**
  - `model_id` (catalog name or internal ID; e.g., llama-3.1-70b-instruct)
  - `model_family` (enum)
  - `model_revision` (hash/tag)
  - `quantization_profile` (fp16/bf16/int8/etc)
  - `gpu_sku_normalized`
  - `gpu_cc` (compute capability, if available)
  - `cuda_version` (major.minor)
  - `driver_version` (major.minor)

- **Workload features used in decision**
  - `seq_bucket` (short, medium, long, xlong; per v2 spec)
  - `prefill_tokens_est` (integer; derived from request metadata)
  - `decode_tokens_max` (integer; from generation config)
  - `scheduler_hint_seq_p50/p90` (if provided; optional)
  - `batch_size_effective` (integer; optional if easily available)

- **Decision outcome**
  - `chosen_kernel` (registry ID)
  - `chosen_kernel_variant` (optional; e.g., flash-attn-v3 vs v2)
  - `fallback_reason` (enum)
  - `selection_error` (string; only if error path, sanitized)
  - `selection_eval_latency_us`
  - `decision_cache_hit` (true/false) (if we cache decisions by key)

- **Candidates (bounded)**
  - `candidate_kernels_top` (string; comma-separated list; max N=5)
  - `candidate_scores_top` (string; comma-separated; max N=5; optional if score exists)
  - `candidate_pruned_reasons` (string; summarized counts, not per-candidate detail)

PII/Privacy:
- Do NOT emit raw prompts, token strings, user identifiers, IPs, or request headers.
- `model_id` is acceptable (internal).
- `prefill_tokens_est` and `decode_tokens_max` are numeric and safe.

## Sampling policy
We support layered sampling so we can keep baseline overhead low but still capture the “bad stuff.”

### Default (Hosted)
- Emit metrics (A) always.
- Emit per-request event (B) at **1%** sample rate.
- Additionally upsample event to **100%** for:
  - requests that hit `fallback_reason != none`
  - selection errors (panic, missing registry entry, compat mismatch)

### Canary / rollout windows
- Runtime config supports bumping event sample rate to **10–25%** for canary regions/pools.
- Separate knob to upsample long-context buckets:
  - `xlong`: 25%
  - `long`: 10%
  - others: default

### Dedicated/Private defaults
- Default event sample rate **0.1%** (metrics still always on), unless customer enables higher sampling.
- “Break glass” override: allow per-process temporary 100% event sampling for X minutes (guarded by admin-only config + explicit log banner). Implementation can follow-up in ENG-xxxx if not ready.

### Determinism & replayability
- Sampling decision must be deterministic per request ID (hash-based) so we can compare v1/v2 or reproduce selection distribution.
- Proposal: `hash64(trace_id || request_id || selector_version) % 10000 < sample_threshold`.

### Overhead constraints
- Event serialization must be O(1) with bounded allocations.
- Candidates list is capped (N=5) + summarized prune counts.
- All string fields should be short enums/IDs, not verbose descriptions.

## Implementation plan
1) Define schema in runtime telemetry module (single source of truth; enums for fallback reasons and seq buckets).
2) Emit metrics in selector decision point (after decision and after fallback).
3) Emit sampled event:
   - attach to request span if tracing enabled
   - otherwise structured log to existing pipeline (no new sink)
4) Add runtime config knobs:
   - `kernel_selector.telemetry.event_sample_rate` (float)
   - `kernel_selector.telemetry.upsample_on_fallback` (bool, default true)
   - `kernel_selector.telemetry.seq_bucket_overrides` (string map encoded as `bucket=rate` list)
   - `kernel_selector.telemetry.max_candidates_logged` (int, default 5)
5) Tests:
   - unit test: sampling determinism given fixed request_id/trace_id
   - unit test: candidate truncation
   - regression test: metrics label set bounded (no model_id label in metrics)

## Acceptance criteria
- [x] Metrics exist and are queryable with bounded label set (no `model_id` in metrics).
- [x] Sampled per-request event includes chosen kernel + inputs needed to explain decision.
- [x] Default sampling does not cause measurable perf regression (selector overhead + log overhead in microbench).
- [x] Canary overrides supported via config and validated in staging.
- [x] Privacy constraints met (no prompt/header/user content).

## Links
- Epic: ENG-4821
- PR: https://sources/github/redwood/pr-28466-add-kernel-selection-telemetry-events.json
- Dashboard work (depends on this data contract): https://sources/github/observability-pack/pr-522-kernel-selection-v2-dashboard-panels.json
- Determinism standards: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/performance-standards/kernel-selection-determinism-and-telemetry-standards.json

## Notes / decisions
- We will not emit per-model metrics labels due to cardinality; per-model analysis should use sampled events + offline queries.
- GPU SKU must use normalized mapping (align with ENG-4823) to avoid instance-name cardinality blowups.
- Candidate scores are optional; if selector is rule-based without numeric scoring, we only emit ordered candidates and prune summary.

## Follow-ups
- Add explicit “debug header” / CLI flag to force event emission for a single request (ties into runtime CLI work).
- Consider adding periodic (e.g., 1/min) summary log per worker with selection distribution to aid Dedicated/Private debugging when traces are unavailable.

title: Top cost drivers service: compute ‘what changed’ deltas + explainability strings
preview:
Build the backend computation for Usage Insights “Top cost drivers” and “What changed?” views.

We need deterministic, non-misleading explanations for cost changes for a given scope (workspace/project, time range, filters), primarily broken down by (route, model bucket), with additional drivers (tokens in/out, cache hit rate, retries, fallbacks, streaming overhead, context length distribution, latency-correlated spend).

Deliverables:
- Heuristic definitions + guardrails (thresholds, confidence, suppression rules)
- Driver ranking algorithm (contribution to $ delta)
- Explainability strings (customer-facing copy templates + evidence metrics)
- Experiments/feature flags for tuning thresholds & copy
- Unit tests + fixtures for common scenarios

Primary consumer: Usage Insights Query API (ENG-3814) and Console UI cards (DES-913 / ENG-3815).

- Brainstorm: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/console-and-tooling/top-cost-drivers-heuristics-brainstorm.json
- PRD: sources/confluence/product-docs/requirements/in-console-usage-insights-prd.json
- ADR (route normalization/attribution): sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-route-normalization-and-attribution.json

title: Usage Insights: cardinality guardrails + sampling for route/model breakdowns
preview:
Problem
Usage Insights requires grouping usage/cost by route + model. Without guardrails, the route dimension can explode (e.g., untemplated IDs, user-defined paths, OpenAI-compat proxies), driving:
- warehouse costs (scan + group-by)
- slow/timeout queries for 7–30d ranges
- noisy/misleading “top drivers” (long tail dominates)
- ingestion issues (high-cardinality tags)

This ticket implements guardrails across ingestion/aggregation/query to keep cardinality bounded while preserving useful reporting.

Goals
1) Prevent route dimension explosion in the aggregated dataset and query service.
2) Handle untemplated or unknown paths safely (bucket + visibility).
3) Cap query complexity so the console remains interactive (and export remains reliable).
4) Add alerts so we detect regressions early (new route patterns, normalization failures, abusive queries, etc.).

Non-goals
- Changing the canonical route normalization rules themselves (owned by ENG-3813 / ADR).
- Building per-customer custom dimensions beyond the fixed set.

Proposed guardrails (implementation)
A) Ingestion + normalization hardening
- Enforce use of normalized_route from the shared library (ENG-3813) at the point of metering event creation (ENG-3822).
- Introduce a dedicated field: route_group (string) used for analytics, which is:
  - the normalized templated route if normalization succeeds (e.g., "/v1/files/{file_id}")
  - otherwise one of:
    - "/__unknown_route" (missing/empty)
    - "/__untemplated" (detected dynamic segment w/o template)
    - "/__invalid" (malformed)
- Add boolean flags on event -> aggregation input:
  - route_is_templated
  - route_is_openai_compat (best-effort; based on gateway route map)

Notes
- We must keep raw route_path out of the aggregation key. Raw route is allowed only in debug logs (sampled) and never in the warehouse grouping key.

B) Aggregation dataset constraints (ENG-3812 integration)
- Aggregate key uses route_group, not raw path.
- Additional constraints at aggregation time:
  - Cap distinct route_group per workspace+day at N (proposal: N=2,000). If exceeded, automatically bucket long-tail routes into "/__other_routes" with route_overflow_count incremented.
  - For each workspace+day+model_family, optionally store TopK routes (proposal: K=200) + “other” bucket.
- Emit dataset-level metrics:
  - distinct_route_group_count{workspace_id, bucket=hour|day}
  - route_overflow_triggered_count
  - route_unknown_count, route_untemplated_count
  - percent_routes_untemplated

Rationale
The Console breakdown is a “top costs” UI; TopK + other maintains accurate totals while preventing a pathological group-by.

C) Query service guardrails (ENG-3814 integration)
Hard limits (server-side; applies to console + API + export initiation):
- time range max:
  - interactive endpoints: 31 days
  - export endpoints: 90 days (async only) with explicit warnings and rate limiting
- max group-by dimensions per request: 2 (route_group, model_family/model_version_bucket). Additional dimensions allowed only if they are low-cardinality and explicitly whitelisted (deployment_mode, region) and only one of them can be added at a time.
- max page size: 500 rows (default 100)
- max “include breakdown details” toggles: reject requests that request per-route per-model per-region per-project simultaneously.

- If a request hits a complexity threshold (estimated by planner), return 422 with a structured error explaining how to narrow (shorter range, drop dimension, filter to a model/route first).
- For console UI: we should fail gracefully with a “Too much data” banner and suggested filters, not a generic error.

- Complexity score = (days * dims_multiplier * estimated_distinct_routes * estimated_distinct_models)

We still need visibility into what is causing route explosions.
- Add a sampled debug log stream (e.g., 0.1% per workspace, capped) that logs raw route_path + normalized_route + reason for bucketing.
- The sampled logs are for internal debugging only and must not be exposed in customer-facing exports.

Add alerts (paging vs ticket) with thresholds tuned during beta:
- distinct_route_group_count spike (e.g., >2x weekly baseline) for a workspace or globally
- percent_routes_untemplated > 5% for >30 minutes (global) OR >10% for a specific workspace
- route_overflow_triggered_count > 0 for any workspace in the last hour (ticket during beta; page only if sustained)
- query_rejected_complexity_count spikes
- p95 query latency correlated with high distinct route counts (for diagnosing)

- Query service doc: sources/confluence/eng-platform/systems-and-services/usage-insights-query-service.json
- Data freshness/quality plan: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/usage-insights-data-freshness-and-quality.json
- Related support pain: SUP-24827 (missing route breakdown for OpenAI-compat), SUP-24863 (timeouts for 30-day range)

Acceptance criteria

- Distinct route groups are capped (TopK + “other” bucketing) without changing totals.
- Query API rejects overly complex requests deterministically with a user-actionable error.
- Console can render the route/model breakdown table for 30 days for a typical enterprise workspace with p95 < SLO target (see ENG-3821) and without timeouts.
- Alerts exist for route cardinality spikes, untemplated routes, overflow bucketing, and complexity rejections.

- Beta: enable for internal + allowlist accounts first; watch distinct_route_group_count and overflow.
- GA: enable globally once thresholds are stable and we have validated totals vs billing (ENG-3817).

- slow/timeout queries for 7–30d ranges
- noisy/misleading “top drivers” (long tail dominates)
- ingestion issues (high-cardinality tags)

This ticket implements guardrails across ingestion/aggregation/query to keep cardinality bounded while preserving useful reporting.

Goals
1) Prevent route dimension explosion in the aggregated dataset and query service.
2) Handle untemplated or unknown paths safely (bucket + visibility).
3) Cap query complexity so the console remains interactive (and export remains reliable).
4) Add alerts so we detect regressions early (new route patterns, normalization failures, abusive queries, etc.).

Non-goals
- Changing the canonical route normalization rules themselves (owned by ENG-3813 / ADR).
- Building per-customer custom dimensions beyond the fixed set.

Proposed guardrails (implementation)
A) Ingestion + normalization hardening
- Enforce use of normalized_route from the shared library (ENG-3813) at the point of metering event creation (ENG-3822).
- Introduce a dedicated field: route_group (string) used for analytics, which is:
  - the normalized templated route if normalization succeeds (e.g., "/v1/files/{file_id}")
  - otherwise one of:
    - "/__unknown_route" (missing/empty)
    - "/__untemplated" (detected dynamic segment w/o template)
    - "/__invalid" (malformed)
- Add boolean flags on event -> aggregation input:
  - route_is_templated
  - route_is_openai_compat (best-effort; based on gateway route map)

- We must keep raw route_path out of the aggregation key. Raw route is allowed only in debug logs (sampled) and never in the warehouse grouping key.

- Additional constraints at aggregation time:
  - Cap distinct route_group per workspace+day at N (proposal: N=2,000). If exceeded, automatically bucket long-tail routes into "/__other_routes" with route_overflow_count incremented.
  - For each workspace+day+model_family, optionally store TopK routes (proposal: K=200) + “other” bucket.

  - distinct_route_group_count{workspace_id, bucket=hour|day}
  - route_overflow_triggered_count
  - route_unknown_count, route_untemplated_count
  - percent_routes_untemplated

The Console breakdown is a “top costs” UI; TopK + other maintains accurate totals while preventing a pathological group-by.

title: Update API gateway timeouts/retries and circuit breakers so failover doesn’t amplify thundering herd
preview:
Context
During regional impairment + automated routing failover, our current gateway behavior can multiply load:
- clients retry (often aggressively) on timeouts/5xx
- gateway retries upstream on a subset of failures
- routing may redirect to another region while the original region is partially impaired

Net: thundering herd risk and cascading overload on the target region, plus extended tail latency while the system is trying to be “helpful”. This is a recurring pattern in incidents and showed up during rehearsals as elevated retries + timeouts right when failover begins.

Goal
Tune gateway retry/timeouts + circuit breaker behavior so the gateway fails fast and predictably during impairment, and so we shed load in a way that cooperates with routing/failover automation.

Non-goals
- Rewriting client SDK retry behavior (tracked separately in docs/SDK follow-ups)
- Changing customer SLA language
- Implementing routing policy logic in gateway (routing remains the source of truth)

Success criteria / acceptance tests
1) Retry amplification bounded
- For non-idempotent generation requests, gateway performs 0 automatic retries by default.
- For idempotent requests (explicit idempotency key) and safe endpoints, at most 1 retry on connect/reset/early upstream termination.
- Retry budget is enforced per request and cannot exceed configured wall-clock limit.

2) Fail-fast behavior during regional impairment
- Circuit breakers open quickly on sustained upstream timeouts/5xx to avoid queue buildup.
- When circuit is open or overload shed triggers, gateway returns a deterministic response with Retry-After.

3) Failover-friendly responses
- Responses during overload / impairment do not encourage immediate synchronized retries.
- Gateway does not “spin” between regions or repeatedly attempt the impaired upstream when routing is actively draining/redirecting traffic.

4) Observability
- Dashboardable metrics for: upstream timeouts, retry attempts, circuit state transitions, shed responses, Retry-After usage.
- Ability to correlate spikes to failover events (via annotations from controller / incident-bot).

Implementation plan (high level)
A) Retry policy changes
- Default: disable gateway retries for generation/chat POSTs unless request includes an idempotency key (Idempotency-Key header) AND failure mode is retry-safe.
- Retry-safe failures: connect timeout, TCP reset, upstream unavailable before request body fully sent.
- No retries on 4xx, no retries on 5xx once response headers started, no retries on explicit overload responses.
- Enforce per-request retry budget (max attempts + max time) and jittered backoff.

B) Timeout tuning
- Separate timeouts for:
  1) connect timeout
  2) upstream headers / first-token timeout (streaming)
  3) upstream idle timeout between chunks (streaming)
  4) total request timeout (non-streaming)
- Tighten defaults so we fail fast during brownouts and let routing take over rather than waiting long enough to stack work.

Proposed defaults (subject to staging validation)
- Connect timeout: 250–500ms (region-local) / 750ms (cross-region)
- Non-streaming upstream total timeout: 10s (was effectively higher for certain routes)
- Streaming:
  - time to first byte / first token: 2.5s default (tier-specific overrides allowed)
  - idle between chunks: 10s (prevents hanging sockets from tying up workers)

C) Circuit breakers / outlier detection
- Per-upstream-cluster circuit breaker keyed by (service, region, route class).
- Trip criteria uses a rolling window (short) and burn-rate style signal:
  - open when timeout rate OR 5xx rate crosses threshold AND corroborated by at least one additional symptom (e.g., upstream queue depth / connection failures) where available.
- Half-open with limited probe concurrency, jittered, and immediate re-open on failure.
- Outlier detection: eject consistently failing upstream hosts to reduce head-of-line blocking.

D) Load shedding + Retry-After
- When shed triggers (local overload or target region saturation), return 429/503 with Retry-After (and a stable error code) rather than letting clients hang until timeout.
- Standardize response semantics so SDKs can back off (ties into runtime + docs work).

E) Guardrails during failover
- If routing/control plane indicates region drain/redirect in progress (header/metadata from routing layer), gateway should avoid retrying the “old” region and should fail fast to allow the client to follow redirect or to allow routing to select a healthy region.
- Ensure we do not create redirect loops; track with a hop counter header (max 1 hop at gateway level).

Testing
- Staging:
  - synthetic impairment: inject upstream 5xx + latency, validate breaker opens and retry attempts remain bounded
  - load test: verify no worker pool exhaustion from hanging streams
  - regression: ensure normal p50/p95 unaffected for healthy region
- Production (game day support):
  - enable behind feature flag for a limited tenant set (internal + a few low-risk tenants)
  - monitor: retries, timeouts, shed rate, circuit transitions, customer error rate
  - rollback: flag off, restore previous timeout defaults

Rollout
1) Ship code behind flags (gateway.retry_policy_v2, gateway.streaming_timeouts_v2, gateway.circuit_breaker_v2)
2) Staging burn-in 48h
3) Prod canary (1% of Hosted API traffic, internal tenants)
4) Expand to 10% + Dedicated shared gateway paths where applicable
5) Make defaults for Hosted API; keep override for Dedicated per contract/tier

Links
- Related PRs:
  - https://sources/github/redwood/pr-19820-api-gateway-fallback-timeout-tuning.json
  - https://sources/github/redwood/pr-19827-add-graceful-degradation-retry-after.json
  - (client streaming reconnect companion) https://sources/github/redwood/pr-19821-streaming-reconnect-on-redirect.json
- Initiative overview: https://sources/linear/engineering/ENG-2411-regional-failover-automation-upgrade.json
- Burn-rate alerts: https://sources/linear/engineering/ENG-2418-add-failover-metrics-and-slo-burn-alerts.json

Open questions (resolved in comments)
- Whether to allow 1 retry for streaming starts without an idempotency key (decision: no; require explicit idempotency to avoid duplicate generations)
- How aggressive first-token timeout can be without hurting long-context workloads (decision: tiered override + route class; default tightened but not extreme)

title: Marketplace entitlement verification API (AWS/GCP) for subscription → org provisioning flow
preview:
## Context
Marketplace buyers currently hit a confusing failure mode in the first 30–90 minutes after subscription: Redwood Console can’t reliably determine whether the customer is entitled (and to what SKU limits), especially when entitlement propagation is delayed or when the customer already has a Redwood org.

This ticket implements/extends control-plane API endpoints used by the marketplace onboarding entrypoint to:
1) verify a buyer’s AWS/GCP marketplace subscription is active and mapped to a Redwood SKU
2) return a stable entitlement object (or a clear, retryable error) that Console + provisioning can act on
3) support edge cases (existing org linking, multiple subscriptions, delayed propagation)

This is a critical dependency for the marketplace refresh launch (time-to-first-token + reduced support escalations).

## Goals / Non-goals
### Goals
- Provide a single verification API surface for both AWS and GCP.
- Standardize error codes/messages so Console can show actionable UI states.
- Keep the source-of-truth in our entitlement DB (fed by sync job), while allowing on-demand refresh when safe.
- Add audit-grade logging for verification attempts and outcomes (no secrets).

### Non-goals (for this ticket)
- Rewriting the entitlement sync pipeline (tracked in ENG-22116).
- Implementing billing dimension mapping (tracked in ENG-22111).
- Console UI work (tracked in ENG-22112).

## Proposed API surface
All endpoints are under control-plane ("api.redwoodinference.com") and require Redwood user auth (session/JWT) unless otherwise noted.

### 1) Verify entitlement (primary)
**POST** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements:verify`

**Request fields (conceptual)**
- `provider`: `aws` | `gcp`
- `subscription_id`: provider-specific subscription identifier (AWS customer identifier / agreement id; GCP entitlement id)
- `account_id`: provider account/project identifier (AWS payer/account; GCP billing account/project) (optional but recommended)
- `org_id`: Redwood org being linked (optional; required for "link" flows)

**Response**
- `status`: `entitled` | `not_entitled` | `pending` | `blocked`
- `entitlement_id`: Redwood internal entitlement id (when available)
- `sku`: marketplace SKU identifier (e.g., `ri.hosted.usage`, `ri.dedicated.reserved`, `ri.private.license`, + add-ons)
- `limits`: derived limits/quotas for onboarding display (high-level; no billing internals)
- `effective_at`, `expires_at` (if contract/term-based)
- `next_action`: `link_org` | `create_org` | `retry` | `contact_support`

### 2) Fetch entitlement by provider identifier (support/debug + Console polling)
**GET** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements/{provider}/{subscription_id}`
- Returns the last-known entitlement record from our DB (including `last_synced_at`).

### 3) Link entitlement to org (explicit step)
**POST** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements:link`
- Requires user has admin role on `org_id`.
- Idempotent: linking the same entitlement to the same org is a no-op.
- If entitlement is already linked to a different org, return a deterministic error for Console (see error codes).

## Error codes / Console-facing semantics
Return structured errors with a stable `code` and human-safe `message`.

Required codes:
- `ENTITLEMENT_PENDING_PROPAGATION`: No entitlement found yet; recommend retry after N minutes.
- `ENTITLEMENT_NOT_FOUND`: Verified lookup completed but no active entitlement exists.
- `ENTITLEMENT_INACTIVE_OR_EXPIRED`: Entitlement exists but is not active.
- `ENTITLEMENT_ALREADY_LINKED`: Entitlement is linked to a different org.
- `PROVIDER_ID_MISMATCH`: Subscription id does not match account/project context.
- `PROVIDER_UNAVAILABLE`: Provider API timeout/unavailable (only if we attempt on-demand provider validation).
- `SYNC_STALE_TRY_AGAIN`: DB record exists but is older than staleness threshold and refresh attempt failed.
- `UNAUTHORIZED_ORG_ADMIN_REQUIRED`: user lacks permission to link.

Console mapping guidance (to share w/ Chloe/Design):
- `PENDING_PROPAGATION` and `SYNC_STALE_TRY_AGAIN` are retryable with clear CTA and expected timing.
- `ALREADY_LINKED` should direct user to contact support with entitlement id.

## Implementation notes
### Data sources
- Primary: `marketplace_entitlements` table (populated by entitlement sync job).
- Optional on-demand refresh:
  - If `last_synced_at` older than threshold (proposed: 10 minutes during onboarding), we may enqueue a sync for that provider+subscription and immediately return `pending` with `next_action=retry`.
  - Avoid synchronous calls to provider APIs on the request path unless we have strict timeouts + caching; preference is enqueue refresh + return.

### Idempotency + concurrency
- `:link` endpoint must be idempotent; use transaction with unique constraint on `entitlement_id`.
- Protect against double-linking in multi-tab onboarding.

### Authz
- Verify caller is a Redwood user.
- For link operations: require org admin.
- Do not expose provider raw contract details; only return minimal SKU + status + timestamps.

### Logging / audit
- Emit audit event: `marketplace.entitlement.verify` with fields: provider, subscription_id_hash (hash), org_id (if present), result status, error code, latency_ms.
- Emit audit event: `marketplace.entitlement.link` with entitlement_id, org_id, actor_user_id, outcome.

### Telemetry hooks
Coordinate event names w/ Nadia Rahman (ENG-22114):
- `mp_verify_start`, `mp_verify_result` (status + error_code)
- `mp_link_result`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] POST `:verify` returns deterministic status (`entitled`/`pending`/`not_entitled`/`blocked`) for AWS and GCP test subscriptions.
- [ ] GET entitlement endpoint returns last-known record including `last_synced_at`.
- [ ] POST `:link` is idempotent and enforces org-admin permission.
- [ ] Error codes above are implemented and documented in internal API docs.
- [ ] Unit + integration tests cover:
  - pending propagation
  - inactive/expired
  - already-linked
  - multi-subscription per org
  - stale sync record triggering refresh enqueue
- [ ] Audit logs/events are emitted and visible in staging.

## Rollout plan
1) Ship behind feature flag `marketplace_entitlement_verification_v2` (default off).
2) Enable in staging; run end-to-end test plan from Partnerships (AWS/GCP).
3) Enable for internal dogfood orgs + a small % of marketplace onboarding traffic.
4) Full rollout after 48h with no increase in provisioning-delay tickets.

## Notes from partner + support feedback
- Support escalation SUP-4821 indicates customers can wait ~45 minutes for entitlement propagation; Console must show "pending" state with guidance.
- We must avoid wording that implies Redwood can see full marketplace billing details; keep responses minimal.

## Open questions
- What is the canonical AWS identifier we can reliably request from users in Console (CustomerIdentifier vs AgreementId)? (need alignment w/ Partnerships)
- For GCP, do we require both entitlement id and project id, or is entitlement id sufficient for verification?
- Staleness threshold during onboarding: 10 minutes vs 5 minutes?

## Links
- Design spec (subscription → provisioning flow): sources/confluence/product-docs/design-specs/marketplace-subscription-to-org-provisioning-flow.json
- Instrumentation task: ENG-22114
- Console entrypoint task: ENG-22112
- Sync hardening task: ENG-22116

## Context
Marketplace buyers currently hit a confusing failure mode in the first 30–90 minutes after subscription: Redwood Console can’t reliably determine whether the customer is entitled (and to what SKU limits), especially when entitlement propagation is delayed or when the customer already has a Redwood org.

This ticket implements/extends control-plane API endpoints used by the marketplace onboarding entrypoint to:
1) verify a buyer’s AWS/GCP marketplace subscription is active and mapped to a Redwood SKU
2) return a stable entitlement object (or a clear, retryable error) that Console + provisioning can act on
3) support edge cases (existing org linking, multiple subscriptions, delayed propagation)

This is a critical dependency for the marketplace refresh launch (time-to-first-token + reduced support escalations).

## Goals / Non-goals
### Goals
- Provide a single verification API surface for both AWS and GCP.
- Standardize error codes/messages so Console can show actionable UI states.
- Keep the source-of-truth in our entitlement DB (fed by sync job), while allowing on-demand refresh when safe.
- Add audit-grade logging for verification attempts and outcomes (no secrets).

### Non-goals (for this ticket)
- Rewriting the entitlement sync pipeline (tracked in ENG-22116).
- Implementing billing dimension mapping (tracked in ENG-22111).
- Console UI work (tracked in ENG-22112).

## Proposed API surface
All endpoints are under control-plane ("api.redwoodinference.com") and require Redwood user auth (session/JWT) unless otherwise noted.

### 1) Verify entitlement (primary)
**POST** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements:verify`

**Request fields (conceptual)**
- `provider`: `aws` | `gcp`
- `subscription_id`: provider-specific subscription identifier (AWS customer identifier / agreement id; GCP entitlement id)
- `account_id`: provider account/project identifier (AWS payer/account; GCP billing account/project) (optional but recommended)
- `org_id`: Redwood org being linked (optional; required for "link" flows)

**Response**
- `status`: `entitled` | `not_entitled` | `pending` | `blocked`
- `entitlement_id`: Redwood internal entitlement id (when available)
- `sku`: marketplace SKU identifier (e.g., `ri.hosted.usage`, `ri.dedicated.reserved`, `ri.private.license`, + add-ons)
- `limits`: derived limits/quotas for onboarding display (high-level; no billing internals)
- `effective_at`, `expires_at` (if contract/term-based)
- `next_action`: `link_org` | `create_org` | `retry` | `contact_support`

### 2) Fetch entitlement by provider identifier (support/debug + Console polling)
**GET** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements/{provider}/{subscription_id}`
- Returns the last-known entitlement record from our DB (including `last_synced_at`).

### 3) Link entitlement to org (explicit step)
**POST** `/v1/marketplace/entitlements:link`
- Requires user has admin role on `org_id`.
- Idempotent: linking the same entitlement to the same org is a no-op.
- If entitlement is already linked to a different org, return a deterministic error for Console (see error codes).

## Error codes / Console-facing semantics
Return structured errors with a stable `code` and human-safe `message`.

Required codes:
- `ENTITLEMENT_PENDING_PROPAGATION`: No entitlement found yet; recommend retry after N minutes.
- `ENTITLEMENT_NOT_FOUND`: Verified lookup completed but no active entitlement exists.
- `ENTITLEMENT_INACTIVE_OR_EXPIRED`: Entitlement exists but is not active.
- `ENTITLEMENT_ALREADY_LINKED`: Entitlement is linked to a different org.
- `PROVIDER_ID_MISMATCH`: Subscription id does not match account/project context.
- `PROVIDER_UNAVAILABLE`: Provider API timeout/unavailable (only if we attempt on-demand provider validation).
- `SYNC_STALE_TRY_AGAIN`: DB record exists but is older than staleness threshold and refresh attempt failed.
- `UNAUTHORIZED_ORG_ADMIN_REQUIRED`: user lacks permission to link.

Console mapping guidance (to share w/ Chloe/Design):
- `PENDING_PROPAGATION` and `SYNC_STALE_TRY_AGAIN` are retryable with clear CTA and expected timing.
- `ALREADY_LINKED` should direct user to contact support with entitlement id.

## Implementation notes
### Data sources
- Primary: `marketplace_entitlements` table (populated by entitlement sync job).
- Optional on-demand refresh:
  - If `last_synced_at` older than threshold (proposed: 10 minutes during onboarding), we may enqueue a sync for that provider+subscription and immediately return `pending` with `next_action=retry`.
  - Avoid synchronous calls to provider APIs on the request path unless we have strict timeouts + caching; preference is enqueue refresh + return.

### Idempotency + concurrency
- `:link` endpoint must be idempotent; use transaction with unique constraint on `entitlement_id`.
- Protect against double-linking in multi-tab onboarding.

### Authz
- Verify caller is a Redwood user.
- For link operations: require org admin.
- Do not expose provider raw contract details; only return minimal SKU + status + timestamps.

### Logging / audit
- Emit audit event: `marketplace.entitlement.verify` with fields: provider, subscription_id_hash (hash), org_id (if present), result status, error code, latency_ms.
- Emit audit event: `marketplace.entitlement.link` with entitlement_id, org_id, actor_user_id, outcome.

### Telemetry hooks
Coordinate event names w/ Nadia Rahman (ENG-22114):
- `mp_verify_start`, `mp_verify_result` (status + error_code)
- `mp_link_result`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] POST `:verify` returns deterministic status (`entitled`/`pending`/`not_entitled`/`blocked`) for AWS and GCP test subscriptions.
- [ ] GET entitlement endpoint returns last-known record including `last_synced_at`.
- [ ] POST `:link` is idempotent and enforces org-admin permission.
- [ ] Error codes above are implemented and documented in internal API docs.
- [ ] Unit + integration tests cover:
  - pending propagation
  - inactive/expired
  - already-linked
  - multi-subscription per org
  - stale sync record triggering refresh enqueue
- [ ] Audit logs/events are emitted and visible in staging.

## Rollout plan
1) Ship behind feature flag `marketplace_entitlement_verification_v2` (default off).
2) Enable in staging; run end-to-end test plan from Partnerships (AWS/GCP).
3) Enable for internal dogfood orgs + a small % of marketplace onboarding traffic.
4) Full rollout after 48h with no increase in provisioning-delay tickets.

## Notes from partner + support feedback
- Support escalation SUP-4821 indicates customers can wait ~45 minutes for entitlement propagation; Console must show "pending" state with guidance.
- We must avoid wording that implies Redwood can see full marketplace billing details; keep responses minimal.

## Open questions
- What is the canonical AWS identifier we can reliably request from users in Console (CustomerIdentifier vs AgreementId)? (need alignment w/ Partnerships)
- For GCP, do we require both entitlement id and project id, or is entitlement id sufficient for verification?
- Staleness threshold during onboarding: 10 minutes vs 5 minutes?

## Links
- Design spec (subscription → provisioning flow): sources/confluence/product-docs/design-specs/marketplace-subscription-to-org-provisioning-flow.json
- Instrumentation task: ENG-22114
- Console entrypoint task: ENG-22112
- Sync hardening task: ENG-22116

title: Route normalization library (templating IDs) shared by Gateway + Telemetry
preview:
Problem
- Usage Insights requires cost/usage breakdown by API route. Today we emit inconsistent route strings depending on where the event is produced (gateway vs downstream services) and whether a request hit OpenAI-compat vs Redwood-native endpoints.
- Unnormalized paths with IDs (e.g. /v1/workspaces/ws_abc123/projects/prj_9f.../keys/key_...) explode cardinality and make aggregations expensive/unreliable.

Goal
- Ship a single, shared route normalization implementation used by both:
  1) API Gateway request metering/telemetry emitters
  2) Telemetry ingestion/aggregation jobs (as a fallback / validation layer)
- Normalize routes by templating path params (IDs) and standardizing prefixes so aggregation keys are stable.
- Provide tests + a migration/rollout plan to avoid breaking billing reconciliation and existing dashboards.

Non-goals (Phase 1)
- Not a general-purpose URL parser for arbitrary customer-defined routes.
- Not auto-discovering templates from traffic (we will use a controlled template set + a conservative heuristic fallback).
- Not changing billing semantics; only adding/standardizing dimensions used for analytics.

Proposed output fields (event tags)
- route_raw: exact request path from gateway (no query string); retained temporarily for debugging.
- route_template: normalized template used for analytics (e.g. /v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/projects/{project_id}/api-keys/{key_id}).
- route_group: canonical grouping (mostly equals route_template; may collapse minor variants like trailing slash).
- route_source: gateway | ingestion (for auditing/coverage metrics).

Normalization rules (Phase 1)
1) Strip query string and fragment.
   - /v1/chat/completions?stream=true -> /v1/chat/completions
2) Remove trailing slash (except root).
   - /v1/embeddings/ -> /v1/embeddings
3) Prefer exact match against a maintained template allowlist for Redwood + OpenAI-compat.
   Examples (expected route_template):
   - /v1/chat/completions -> /v1/chat/completions
   - /v1/embeddings -> /v1/embeddings
   - /v1/rerank -> /v1/rerank
   - /openai/v1/chat/completions -> /openai/v1/chat/completions
   - /openai/v1/embeddings -> /openai/v1/embeddings
4) For templated routes with IDs, replace segments in known positions.
   - /v1/workspaces/ws_2kQ1/projects/prj_A91/deployments/dpl_77/logs -> /v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/projects/{project_id}/deployments/{deployment_id}/logs
   - /v1/keys/key_abc123 -> /v1/keys/{key_id}
5) Conservative heuristic fallback for unknown routes:
   - If a segment matches common ID patterns (uuid v4, base58-ish tokens >= 16 chars, ws_/prj_/dpl_ prefixes), replace with {id}.
   - If heuristic would replace >2 segments OR the result would be too generic, return /unknown (and keep route_raw for debugging).

Rationale
- Using an allowlist first prevents over-templating and avoids grouping distinct endpoints under the same template.
- Heuristic fallback covers missed templates while limiting cardinality blowups.
- Keeping route_raw temporarily unblocks debugging and customer support cases (e.g. “missing OpenAI route breakdown”) without trusting it for aggregation keys.

Implementation plan
- Create shared package: go module "github.com/redwoodinference/platform/route-normalization" (exact path TBD by repo conventions).
- Expose stable API:
  - Normalize(path string) -> {route_template, route_group, normalization_reason}
  - NormalizeFromURL(url string) -> same, with query stripping
  - Metrics helpers: normalization coverage counters (matched_allowlist, matched_template, heuristic, unknown)
- Add template registry (versioned): templates.go + templates_test.go
  - Explicit test fixtures for every supported route.
- Integrate in Gateway emitter:
  - Emit route_template + route_group on every metering event.
  - Emit route_raw behind a temporary feature flag (default on in beta environments; off in GA unless requested).
- Integrate in Telemetry ingestion:
  - If route_template missing (older agents/regions), compute it at ingestion and set route_source=ingestion.
  - If both present, compute and compare; log mismatch rate with sampling.

Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Allowlist exact matches (Redwood + OpenAI-compat)
  - ID templating for all “ID-bearing” endpoints
  - Query stripping / trailing slash
  - Heuristic behavior (replace 1–2 segments only)
  - Negative tests: do NOT template common nouns (e.g. /v1/models/llama-3.1-70b stays literal if in allowlist)
- Integration tests:
  - Gateway event emission contains stable route_template for representative traffic
  - Ingestion fallback produces identical templates for the same request
- Regression tests:
  - Fixtures sourced from recent gateway logs to ensure no surprises with real traffic patterns

Rollout / migration plan
1) Phase A (1–2 days): library lands + gateway emits route_template + route_group in parallel; ingestion fallback enabled.
2) Phase B (1 week): monitor normalization coverage + mismatch rate dashboards.
   - Target: >= 99% matched_allowlist/template/heuristic (non-unknown) for /v1/* and /openai/v1/*.
   - Mismatch target: < 0.5% sampled mismatches (excluding known legacy paths).
3) Phase C: switch Usage Insights aggregation (ENG-3812) to key on route_group.
4) Phase D: deprecate raw route usage in analytics; keep route_raw only for debug sampling, gated by env + retention.

Risks / open questions
- OpenAI-compat routing: ensure we treat /openai/v1/* as first-class templates; do not collapse into /v1/*.
- Streaming: stream=true is a query param; we strip it so route_template is stable. Streaming overhead will be attributed via separate dimension(s) (not via route).
- Cardinality: heuristic fallback must remain conservative. If unknown traffic increases, we add templates explicitly rather than broadening heuristics.
- Backfill: we will not backfill historical data in Phase 1; insights will show normalized routes from rollout forward. (Backfill plan tracked separately.)

Acceptance criteria
- Shared library merged with full test suite.
- Gateway emits route_template + route_group for 100% of metering events in at least one canary region.
- Ingestion fallback enabled and mismatch rate is within target.
- Template registry includes all current public API endpoints used for Usage Insights (chat, completions, embeddings, rerank + auth/admin endpoints that generate meaningful spend).

Links
- ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-route-normalization-and-attribution.json
- Slack thread (edge cases): sources/slack/eng-platform/1737468804.json
- Related: ENG-3822 (route tag instrumentation), ENG-3812 (aggregation dataset keyed by route/model)

title: Clarify token-source attribution and recovery after shard replay
preview: Summary: During a recent audit of reconstructed usage we observed persistent per-model attribution oscillations after shard replays and targeted backfills. The symptom is that daily peak order and per-model top lists shift unexpectedly after backfill windows complete. Root cause appears to be non-deterministic attribution when rate-limit retry fan-in and replay markers are present: downstream aggregators sometimes favor the replay event's model_alias (or lose the original_model_id) resulting in charge redistribution to 'fallback' model names in per-model charts.

title: Clockless anchoring reset for tenant-granular quotas
preview:
Problem: Tenant quota resets across daily/monthly and rolling windows are fragile in the presence of clock skew, cross-region failover, DST leaps, and reserved capacity handoffs. Existing epoch-based resets rely on UTC or tenant TZ anchors which complicates reserved pool migrations and produces brief double-counting or missed resets for high-throughput tenants.

Goal: Implement a clockless anchoring reset mechanism that uses distributed checkpoints and idempotent reset tokens to produce deterministic, idempotent quota resets for tenant-scoped meters. The mechanism should work for both rolling and fixed-window quotas, be compatible with reserved-capacity leases, and allow fast safe cutovers during migrations.

Approach summary:
- Checkpoint epochs: persist lightweight reset checkpoints to the usage ledger as monotonic markers (reset_token). Checkpoints are produced by the lease owner for a tenant or by a quorum probe when leases are transferred.
- Clockless anchor: instead of relying on wall-clock boundary, the reset is anchored to the checkpoint token sequence number + committed ledger offset. Reset logic uses token idempotency so repeated or concurrent resets converge.
- Lease-aware semantics: when reserved-capacity leases change owners, the takeover process writes a handoff checkpoint and a planned-reset marker. Consumers use these to avoid double resets during in-flight transfers.
- Reconciliation job: background reconcile worker scans ledger to detect missing checkpoints and backfill deterministic resets; reports anomalies to billing telemetry.

Why different from existing solutions: focuses on ledger-driven deterministic resets (token/offset anchored) rather than epoch math. This reduces edge cases during cross-pool migrations, DST edges, and leap seconds, and minimizes customer-visible quota drift.

Success criteria:
- No increase in customer-visible overage incidents during cutovers in canary tenants (target 0 regressions).
- Reconciliation latency median < 2 minutes for missing checkpoint detection.
- End-to-end idempotent reset verified via automated property tests and integration tests using simulated clock skew and lease flapping.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Reconciliation false positives causing force resets. Mitigation: require a quorum of ledger writes and a short jitter window before backfill.
- Risk: Performance overhead on high-throughput tenants. Mitigation: checkpoint writes are sharded and batched; use compact marker records and non-blocking fast-path for common requests.

Implementation plan:
1) RFC and Confluence spec (linked below) describing ledger markers, reset_token format, and idempotency semantics.
2) Low-risk prototype: implement checkpoint write in quota-service with feature flag; add auditor job that reports but does not backfill.
3) Canary rollout to 5 mid-size tenants with monitoring dashboards and rollback feature flag.
4) Enable backfill in reconciliation after 1 week of clean signals.
5) Full rollout and deprecation of older epoch anchoring for new tenants, with a migration path for existing reserved pools.

Testing:
- Unit tests for token idempotency and edge-case merges.
- Integration tests that spin up 3 ledger replicas, simulate lease transfers, DST and leap-second events, and validate quota invariants.
- Load tests with synthetic tenants to validate checkpoint write latency under 95th percentile SLA.

Metrics and alerts:
- telemetry.quota.reset_attempts.count (by tenant, status)
- telemetry.quota.reset_idempotency_conflicts.count
- latency.quota.checkpoint_write.p95
- reconciliation.backfill.jobs.duration.p99
- alert if reconciliation anomalies > 0.1% of tenants over 1h window

Docs and PRs: see attachments list for design doc and early PRs.

title: Adaptive setpoint autoscale soak probe (reverse-sawtooth + steady-state)
preview: Create and validate an adaptive 'setpoint' autoscale soak probe that exercises Dedicated capacity under a compound workload: long steady-state baseline plus periodic reverse-sawtooth bursts that ramp down quickly then sustain mid-level traffic. The harness should measure tokens/sec, p95/p99 generation latency, cold-start incidents, and KV cache hit ratio; detect regressions vs a rolling baseline; and produce signals suitable for integration with our autoscaler fuzzing and alerting pipelines. Unlike previous burst/soak harnesses, this probe uses a thermostatic controller to nudge target capacity when p95 drifts above configurable thresholds, emulating an operator-adjusted with hysteresis to avoid oscillation.

title: Run KMS outage game day (timeout/throttling/AccessDenied) + capture follow-ups
preview:
## Goal
Validate end-to-end behavior and operator readiness when KMS is degraded or misconfigured across Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployment modes.

We want to confirm:
- KMS wrapper timeouts/retry budgets/circuit breaker prevent retry storms + cascading failures

## Scenarios (run in order)
### Scenario A — Regional KMS latency + intermittent timeouts (most realistic)
**Injection:** Add 2–4s latency + 10–20% timeout rate for KMS calls in staging (us-east). Duration 20 minutes.
**Expected behavior:**
- Wrapper enforces per-call timeout and bounded retries with jitter
- Circuit breaker opens after threshold; service returns controlled errors (no unbounded queue growth)

### Scenario B — KMS throttling (rate limiting)
**Injection:** Simulate throttling responses and/or reduce KMS quota to force 429/Throttling. Duration 15 minutes.
**Expected behavior:**
- Backoff/jitter observed; no synchronized retry bursts
- Backpressure/throttle budget protects KMS and preserves overall system health
- Error taxonomy shows Throttling, not Timeout, and remediation guidance is consistent

### Scenario C — Permission regression / AccessDenied
**Injection:** Remove a required decrypt permission from the staging role (or swap to a role missing kms:Decrypt). Duration 10 minutes.
**Expected behavior:**
- Fail fast with AccessDenied classification (no retries)
- Operator-facing errors include missing action/principal hints
- Runbook leads on-call to confirm principal/ARN mismatch quickly

**Injection:** Disable the “new” key version while dual-read is enabled; then attempt cutover.
**Expected behavior:**
- Dual-read continues to succeed via old key; cutover is blocked / rollback guidance is clear

title: Attention kernel choosing safety-net and synthetic benchmark simulator
preview:
Problem: We continue to see intermittent latency regressions and memory spikes when the kernel selector flips between FlashAttention v2 and v3 at certain batch/sequence-shape combinations during prefill and decode. Root cause is unclear across cases: some relate to v3 kernel workspace fragmentation, others to startup jitter when speculative warmup probes run.

Goal: Build a synthetic benchmark + simulator that can: 1) reliably reproduce the shape/occupancy conditions that trigger regressions, 2) exercise the kernel selection logic (including hysteresis, gating, and fallback), and 3) produce a set of safety thresholds and automatic rollback triggers for staged rollouts. The simulator should be lightweight, deterministic for CI, and capable of running on CI/GHA runners and on an engineering GPU pool.

Scope: implement workload generator, kernel selector harness (pluggable for v2/v3/quantized variants), telemetry exporters (latency, mem footprint, workspace alloc/reuse stats, kernel switch frequency), regression detector, and an evaluation dashboard export. Deliverables include a reproducible harness, a README with experiment recipes, an initial set of safety thresholds, and a PR with a canary rollout plan tied to runtime-1.19.

title: Quantization load guardrails: qprofile-audit harness for perf/quality tradeoffs
preview: Build a reproducible, auditable benchmarking harness (qprofile-audit) to evaluate FP16/INT8/INT4 quant profiles across representative workloads and detect regressions in tokens/sec, p95 latency, and output quality. The goal is an actionable guardrail that allows model onboarding and automatic rollbacks if a quant profile causes unacceptable quality or latency regressions for production workloads. This ticket covers harness design, dataset selection, statistical detection rules, CI integration, dashboards, and a small pilot run with 2 models.
