title: Dynamic SLI onboarding and traffic escrow policy for prioritized conversational endpoints
preview:
Background: As we onboard more conversational routes and tuned model variants, teams need a predictable way to declare SLIs and safely migrate traffic without risking noisy error-budget burn across regions. This ticket defines a prescriptive onboarding flow that (1) creates per-model SLIs from sampled traffic and synthetic probe signals, (2) allocates an "traffic escrow" (error-budget currency) to new model variants, and (3) wires an automated regional failover choreography tied to the escrow balance and circuit-breaker state. The goal is to reduce rollouts that unexpectedly exhaust global error budgets and to enable short-lived experiments with automatic safe ejection.

Scope:
- Define canonical SLI signals for conversational endpoints (latency P95/P99, request-quality pass rate, token-error-rate, downstream HTTP error rate).
- Specify sampling rates and synthetic probe cadence for initial SLI estimation.
- Design the traffic escrow model: how much traffic/time is allocated to a variant, how escrow is consumed and replenished, and how credits are reconciled across regions.
- Define failover choreography triggered by escrow depletion, circuit open events, or regional capacity pressure (including warm-standby behaviors).
- Produce operational playbook snippets for oncall: what alerts mean, automated remediation steps, and manual escalation checklist.

Out of scope: long-term SLI visualization UI improvements (console work will be a follow-up), and per-tenant economic billing changes.

title: Disconnected-client heuristics and partial-output fanout cleanup
preview:
Problem: When clients disconnect mid-generation we occasionally see duplicated partial payloads, lingering per-generation state in memory, and cross-tenant KV cache pollution during 'fanout' (when a single generation is mirrored to multiple downstream consumers). This causes elevated memory usage, confusing logs that look like duplicate outputs, and complicates function/tool-call coordination for resumed sessions.

Goal: Implement a deterministic, idempotent cleanup and resume protocol that: (1) detects and cordons disconnected clients quickly, (2) marks partial generations with a compact emission fingerprint (EID) to prevent duplicate downstream fanout, (3) quarantines orphaned generation state in a TTL-based soft-quarantine for safe rollback/inspection, and (4) exposes clear metrics and alerts for stale-generation accumulation.

Constraints: Must be backward-compatible with existing SDKs (compat shim for older clients), preserve structured function-call atomicity for resumed sessions, and keep end-to-end latency impact <= 5% at p95 under normal load. Non-goal: redesigning the entire lease/token system or changing cross-region routing policies in this ticket.

title: CI: reproducible model release flow with signed artifacts and profile promotion
preview: Implement a reproducible CI/CD flow that produces versioned model artifacts, cryptographically signs the build outputs and performance profiles, and promotes artifacts through staging -> canary -> prod with automated checks. The goal is to remove manual steps in onboarding new model weights and their associated performance profiles, provide verifiable provenance for sensitive customer deployments, and enable safe, auditable rollouts. Key pieces: 1) deterministic packaging of model artifacts + manifest (weights, tokenizer, quantization metadata, build environment hash); 2) artifact signing and attestation (Sigstore/cosign integration + Rekor transparency); 3) automated perf-profile generation, validation and version publish to the model catalog; 4) promotion workflow (staging acceptance tests, canary rollout with health checks and evaluation harness tie-in, automatic rollback on regressions); 5) integration with Dedicated/Private pinning manifests so deployments reference signed version tags rather than floating refs.

title: Add burst configuration to Control Plane API (tenant enablement, limits, priority tier, validation, audit hooks)
preview:
Context
Dedicated Burst Capacity requires a tenant-scoped configuration surface in the Control Plane so we can:
- enable/disable burst per Dedicated tenant
- set hard caps/guardrails that admission/routing can enforce deterministically
- expose read APIs to Console + support tooling
- produce audit events for all config changes (including admin overrides)

This ticket is specifically the Control Plane API + validation + audit logging hooks. The admission controller and routing integration are tracked separately.

Scope (in)
1) Tenant model/config schema
- Add a BurstConfig block on the tenant (or dedicated capacity config) with:
  - enabled: boolean
  - max_burst_ratio: float (relative to baseline reserved capacity) OR max_burst_gpus: int (absolute cap), with clear precedence rules
  - max_burst_gpus: int (optional)
  - priority_tier: enum (e.g., standard | silver | gold) used only for fairness weighting; must not allow starvation of non-priority tenants
  - cooldown_seconds: int (minimum time after sustained burst usage before admitting additional burst beyond baseline; exact semantics owned by admission controller but validated here)
  - min_burst_deny_retry_after_seconds: int (optional; used for 429 guidance / Retry-After)
  - notes: string (optional, internal-only; for CS/Sales context)

2) Control Plane APIs
- Admin API (internal) to set burst config for a tenant
  - PATCH /internal/v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config
  - GET   /internal/v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config
- Customer-facing read API (used by Console) to display burst enablement + limits
  - GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config (read-only; gated to tenant principals)

API response should include:
- effective_config (after defaults + validation)
- last_updated_at
- last_updated_by (redacted appropriately for customer view)

3) Validation + defaults (server-side)
- enabled default: false
- max_burst_ratio default: 1.5 for beta tenants unless explicitly overridden (safe default; can be adjusted later without schema changes)
- max_burst_ratio allowed range: [1.0, 3.0] (beta); reject outside range
- max_burst_gpus allowed range: [0, 512] with additional guard: cannot exceed fleet safety cap policy for the tenant’s region/hardware class (enforced via policy engine rule; Control Plane validates using policy evaluation results)
- priority_tier allowed values: standard, silver, gold (future-proofed: reject unknown)
- cooldown_seconds allowed range: [0, 3600]
- Precedence: if max_burst_gpus is set, admission uses min(max_burst_gpus, floor(max_burst_ratio * baseline_gpus)); if only ratio is set, cap is ratio * baseline; if neither set, use defaults.
- Reject invalid combinations:
  - enabled=true and max_burst_ratio < 1.0
  - max_burst_gpus set but tenant has no Dedicated baseline (Dedicated-only feature)
  - priority_tier != standard unless tenant has burst enabled

4) Audit logging hooks
- Emit audit events on:
  - burst_config.enabled changed
  - burst_config.max_burst_ratio changed
  - burst_config.max_burst_gpus changed
  - burst_config.priority_tier changed
  - burst_config.cooldown_seconds changed
  - burst_config overridden via emergency / admin override path (include override reason)
- Event requirements:
  - actor (user/service), actor_type, request_id, tenant_id
  - before/after (with safe redaction for internal notes)
  - source: console | api | internal_tool

Out of scope (for this ticket)
- Per-region overrides / cross-region bursting controls (explicitly out of scope for initial release)
- Customer-writable burst config (beta/GA is Redwood-enabled only; customers can view in Console)
- Admission semantics (queue vs immediate deny) beyond what’s needed for API shape

Implementation notes
- Store in tenant config model so routing + admission can consume same source of truth via existing config distribution.
- Avoid high-cardinality audit payloads; keep diffs concise.
- All writes must go through RBAC check (Control Plane)
  - internal write: platform-admin, dedicated-ops
  - customer read: tenant-admin + dedicated-viewer
- Include propagation expectations in response headers (we’ve seen customer confusion when Console lags).
  - Add "x-config-propagation-estimate-seconds" header (best-effort) OR include in response message.

Acceptance criteria
- [x] New BurstConfig fields added to tenant schema + persisted
- [x] PATCH endpoint supports partial updates and validates ranges/combos
- [x] GET endpoints return effective config (defaults applied) and metadata (last updated)
- [x] RBAC enforced for read/write paths
- [x] Audit events emitted for all config changes and admin override
- [x] Backward compatible migration (existing tenants unaffected; enabled=false default)
- [x] Unit tests for validation and RBAC; integration test for audit event emission

text:
Context
Dedicated Burst Capacity requires a tenant-scoped configuration surface in the Control Plane so we can:
- enable/disable burst per Dedicated tenant
- set hard caps/guardrails that admission/routing can enforce deterministically
- expose read APIs to Console + support tooling
- produce audit events for all config changes (including admin overrides)

This ticket is specifically the Control Plane API + validation + audit logging hooks. The admission controller and routing integration are tracked separately.

Scope (in)
1) Tenant model/config schema
- Add a BurstConfig block on the tenant (or dedicated capacity config) with:
  - enabled: boolean
  - max_burst_ratio: float (relative to baseline reserved capacity) OR max_burst_gpus: int (absolute cap), with clear precedence rules
  - max_burst_gpus: int (optional)
  - priority_tier: enum (e.g., standard | silver | gold) used only for fairness weighting; must not allow starvation of non-priority tenants
  - cooldown_seconds: int (minimum time after sustained burst usage before admitting additional burst beyond baseline; exact semantics owned by admission controller but validated here)
  - min_burst_deny_retry_after_seconds: int (optional; used for 429 guidance / Retry-After)
  - notes: string (optional, internal-only; for CS/Sales context)

2) Control Plane APIs
- Admin API (internal) to set burst config for a tenant
  - PATCH /internal/v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config
  - GET   /internal/v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config
- Customer-facing read API (used by Console) to display burst enablement + limits
  - GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/burst-config (read-only; gated to tenant principals)

API response should include:
- effective_config (after defaults + validation)
- last_updated_at
- last_updated_by (redacted appropriately for customer view)

3) Validation + defaults (server-side)
- enabled default: false
- max_burst_ratio default: 1.5 for beta tenants unless explicitly overridden (safe default; can be adjusted later without schema changes)
- max_burst_ratio allowed range: [1.0, 3.0] (beta); reject outside range
- max_burst_gpus allowed range: [0, 512] with additional guard: cannot exceed fleet safety cap policy for the tenant’s region/hardware class (enforced via policy engine rule; Control Plane validates using policy evaluation results)
- priority_tier allowed values: standard, silver, gold (future-proofed: reject unknown)
- cooldown_seconds allowed range: [0, 3600]
- Precedence: if max_burst_gpus is set, admission uses min(max_burst_gpus, floor(max_burst_ratio * baseline_gpus)); if only ratio is set, cap is ratio * baseline; if neither set, use defaults.
- Reject invalid combinations:
  - enabled=true and max_burst_ratio < 1.0
  - max_burst_gpus set but tenant has no Dedicated baseline (Dedicated-only feature)
  - priority_tier != standard unless tenant has burst enabled

4) Audit logging hooks
- Emit audit events on:
  - burst_config.enabled changed
  - burst_config.max_burst_ratio changed
  - burst_config.max_burst_gpus changed
  - burst_config.priority_tier changed
  - burst_config.cooldown_seconds changed
  - burst_config overridden via emergency / admin override path (include override reason)
- Event requirements:
  - actor (user/service), actor_type, request_id, tenant_id
  - before/after (with safe redaction for internal notes)
  - source: console | api | internal_tool

Out of scope (for this ticket)
- Per-region overrides / cross-region bursting controls (explicitly out of scope for initial release)
- Customer-writable burst config (beta/GA is Redwood-enabled only; customers can view in Console)
- Admission semantics (queue vs immediate deny) beyond what’s needed for API shape

Implementation notes
- Store in tenant config model so routing + admission can consume same source of truth via existing config distribution.
- Avoid high-cardinality audit payloads; keep diffs concise.
- All writes must go through RBAC check (Control Plane)
  - internal write: platform-admin, dedicated-ops
  - customer read: tenant-admin + dedicated-viewer
- Include propagation expectations in response headers (we’ve seen customer confusion when Console lags).
  - Add "x-config-propagation-estimate-seconds" header (best-effort) OR include in response message.

- [x] PATCH endpoint supports partial updates and validates ranges/combos
- [x] GET endpoints return effective config (defaults applied) and metadata (last updated)
- [x] RBAC enforced for read/write paths
- [x] Audit events emitted for all config changes and admin override
- [x] Backward compatible migration (existing tenants unaffected; enabled=false default)

title: Filtering engine for SIEM-ready audit exports (allow/deny by category/action + actor/resource predicates)
preview:
Goal
Implement the filtering model for SIEM-ready audit exports to support enterprise requirements (noise reduction, least-data export) while preserving delivery guarantees and avoiding performance regressions.

This ticket covers:
- Runtime evaluation of export filters (exporter-side) for each audit event.
- Validation/normalization rules shared with control plane to prevent unsafe/expensive filters.
- Safe defaults and clear semantics (allowlist + denylist, deny wins).
- Performance tests to ensure filtering overhead is negligible at expected event volumes.

References
- Confluence ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-audit-export-filtering-model.json
- Schema v2 + category taxonomy: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/audit-event-schema-v2-and-siem-mapping.json
- Control plane export config API ticket: ENG-5132
- Exporter scaffold/config sync ticket: ENG-5139

Non-goals (v1)
- Arbitrary regex matching or JSONPath over event payloads (too risky + too expensive + hard to validate).
- Full text search over request/response bodies (prohibited by PII policy anyway).
- Cross-event correlation filtering.

Proposed semantics (v1)
1) Filter structure
- allow:
  - categories: [string]
  - actions: [string]
  - actor_types: ["user"|"service_account"|"api_key"|"system"]
  - resource_types: [string]
- deny:
  - categories/actions/actor_types/resource_types (same shapes as allow)
- predicates (optional):
  - actor_ids: [string] (exact match only; intended for service accounts / API keys)
  - resource_ids: [string] (exact match only)
  - workspace_ids: [string] (exact match)
  - source_ip_cidrs: [cidr] (optional; only evaluated when event has source_ip)

2) Matching rules
- If allow.* is empty / unset: treat as “allow all” for that dimension.
  Example: allow.categories unset means categories do not constrain.
- If allow.* is set and non-empty: event must match at least one value in each provided allow dimension.
  Example: if allow.categories AND allow.actions are set, event must match both.
- Deny is always applied. If an event matches any deny clause, it is excluded even if it matches allow.
- Predicates behave as additional allow constraints when present (i.e., must match).
- Unknown categories/actions are treated as non-matching (conservative) when allow.* is explicitly set.
- Missing optional event fields:
  - If predicate requires field (e.g., source_ip_cidrs) and event lacks it => does not match predicate (excluded if predicate is set).

3) Safe defaults
- Default export behavior when no filter is configured: export all audit events emitted by schema v2 (subject to audit event PII policy / redaction rules; filtering is not a substitute for redaction).
- For beta UI, we will recommend customers start with allow.categories only (least error-prone) and expand as needed.

Validation / guardrails
To prevent pathological configs and keep evaluation cheap:
- Hard limits:
  - max values per list: 100
  - max total values across allow+deny+predicates: 400
  - max CIDRs: 50
- Strings:
  - exact match only (no regex, no wildcards)
  - max length 128
- CIDR validation:
  - IPv4 and IPv6 supported; parsed at config load.
- Normalization:
  - de-duplicate lists
  - lower-case category/action tokens (category taxonomy is lowercase per schema v2)
- Config load failure behavior:
  - If filter fails validation, the exporter should mark destination as “config_error” and NOT export until fixed (avoid accidentally exporting too much).

Implementation notes
- Implemented as a small, allocation-friendly matcher compiled once per (tenant, destination) config refresh.
- Representation:
  - allow/deny token lists stored as hash sets for O(1) lookups.
  - CIDRs compiled to netaddr objects and checked only if event.source_ip exists and source_ip_cidrs is non-empty.
- Evaluation order (fast-fail):
  1) deny tokens (category/action/actor_type/resource_type)
  2) allow tokens
  3) predicate exact matches (workspace_id, actor_id, resource_id)
  4) CIDR predicate
- Observability:
  - exporter increments counters per destination for filtered_out_total with labels {reason=deny|allow_miss|predicate_miss} (label cardinality constrained; no category/action labels).
  - exporter logs per-batch summary at debug (not info) to avoid noisy logs.

Performance tests
Added microbench + load test coverage:
- Bench scenario: 10k events batch, 3 allow dimensions, 1 deny dimension, 10 CIDRs.
- Target: p95 evaluation overhead <= 5ms per 10k events on baseline exporter instance type (filtering should be dwarfed by encoding + network IO).
- Added regression test in CI to fail if overhead increases by >25% vs pinned baseline.

Acceptance criteria
- Export config supports allowlist/denylist by category/action/actor_type/resource_type.
- Export config supports optional predicates for actor_id/resource_id/workspace_id and source_ip CIDRs.
- Deny overrides allow.
- Empty allow dimension does not constrain.
- Exporter behavior on invalid filter is fail-closed (no export) with surfaced status.
- Unit tests cover edge cases (missing fields, unknown categories, empty lists, duplicates).
- Bench tests exist and run in CI (or nightly) with documented thresholds.

Rollout considerations
- Behind SIEM export beta feature flag; matcher compiled only for enabled tenants.
- Requires schema v2 categories/actions to be stable enough for customers to build allowlists; PM to provide canonical category list in docs.

Follow-ups (out of scope here)
- “Preview filter” endpoint in control plane / Console that shows sample counts by category over last N minutes (requires aggregation store).
- Support for “starts_with” on resource_id for hierarchical resource naming (needs careful validation).
- Tenant-level default denylist for known noisy system actions (needs Security review to avoid hiding security-relevant events).

title: Orchestrator peering route propagation & session-thrash follow-up
preview: During a routine orchestrator upgrade on private deployments using cross-account VPC peering, a subset of control plane nodes lost connectivity due to delayed route propagation combined with stale session tokens on agents. This caused repeated leader re-elections, API timeouts for customer control plane actions, and transient capacity loss for several customers in EU-West-1. The incident surface was primarily control plane API errors and agent reconnection storms rather than model-serving compute failures.

title: Embedding quality simulator and telemetry adaptor for offline & drift detection
preview: Build an extensible evaluation harness that (1) synthesizes controlled perturbations to create robust golden test cases, (2) runs nightly offline quality metrics across models/versions, and (3) publishes drift and regression signals into Redwood Console telemetry and alerting. This ticket contains the simulator + adaptor components and a lightweight orchestration job for nightly runs. Goal is to catch embedding quality regressions (semantic flips, topical drift, model regressions) earlier and provide actionable signal (metric + example anchors) for model onboarding and runtime rollouts.

title: Opaque bundle signer and staged traffic shim for profile rollout
preview: Goal: Build a hardened CI/CD flow that produces an opaque, signed model bundle, publishes a corresponding performance profile, and wires a staged traffic shim so new model versions receive controlled traffic during rollouts. This ticket covers the signer service integration, bundle metadata handshake with the model registry, automated profile broadcast to the profile-store, and a small-traffic routing shim plus automatic rollback hooks when eval or telemetry thresholds fail.

title: Per-device performance envelopes and model-pin release workflow
preview: Goal: Define per-device 'performance envelopes' (latency P50/P95, token/sec, memory footprints) for A100/H100/L40S/MI300 variants and codify a model version pinning + publish workflow that ensures safe rollout of new performance profiles to Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployments. This ticket produces a reproducible onboarding checklist, a canonical JSON schema for published profiles, and a gated release process that ties perf profile publication to canary rollout steps and telemetry gating. Scope excludes model compression experiments (separate RFC).

title: Quant artifact anchoring and rollback ledger for reproducible calibration
preview:
Problem: We currently produce FP8/INT8 quant artifacts and calibration snapshots without a single authoritative, signed record that binds the artifact bytes to the calibration inputs, quantization config, toolchain, and model binary. That makes retrospective verification, reproducible rollbacks, and automated safety checks brittle.

Goal: Implement an artifact anchoring ledger and CLI verification flow that: (1) computes deterministic content fingerprints for model binaries, calibration datasets, quant profiles and build metadata; (2) publishes a signed, append-only ledger record per quant release containing the fingerprints, human-readable manifest, and storage pointers; (3) provides a verification tool to replay/validate calibration results and detect bit-rot or untrusted modifications; (4) surface an automated rollback playbook when verification or accuracy validation fails.

Success criteria include deterministic hashing of outputs, signed manifests using KMS-backed keys, nightly automated verification against goldensets, and an operator playbook for rollback that is safe for Dedicated/Private tenants.

title: Tenant-aware fairness simulator for regional scarcity and graceful degradation
preview: Goal: validate that admission control, circuit breakers, and load-shedding policies enforce predictable SLO degradation across tenants when a region experiences progressive GPU capacity loss or dependency silence. We need a reproducible simulator and harness that can: (1) emulate partial region failures (gradual GPU eviction, abrupt node loss, increasing enqueue delays), (2) emulate downstream dependency silence/muting (redis/cache/kv proxies returning timeouts or dropped responses without raising downstream alerts), and (3) measure per-tenant SLOs and fairness metrics under different admission/policy configurations. The output will inform runtime default policies and provide test cases for canary gating of autoscaling and admission improvements.

title: Admin Workshop: Lightweight utilities + runbook-as-task simulator
preview: Problem: on-call and support engineers spend significant time running ad-hoc admin scripts and manually executing multi-step runbooks; current runbooks are hard to test and often have hidden side effects. Goal: deliver a lightweight admin utility library and a local/runtime 'runbook-as-task' simulator that lets engineers author runbook steps as composable tasks, execute dry-runs with synthetic data, and validate safety gates and telemetry before touching production. Scope: 1) small language-agnostic utilities (idempotent helpers, safe wrappers, canned retries) for reuse across adminlets; 2) a simulator binary (iterative prototype in Python) that loads a runbook definition, simulates each step with mock connectors, surfaces side-effect summary, and calculates estimated blast radius; 3) tight observability: emitted structured telemetry events for every simulated step; 4) safety policy hooks (RBAC checks, approval gates, canary rollouts) that can be toggled in sim mode. Out of scope: full UI console integration and production rollout of the simulator in this ticket (follow-ups will wire to console and runbook catalog).

title: Construct latency-utility frontier for stochastic batching and eviction-aware cache tuning
preview: Objective: build an evaluation harness and dashboard to map the latency-utility (quality) frontier across stochastic batching policies and eviction-aware key-value (KV) cache TTL/eviction strategies. We want a compact signal that captures model-answer utility (win-rate and instruction-following) vs end-to-end latency for representative production workloads so we can automatically detect and rank acceptable config variants for rollouts and automated fallback rules.

title: Pinned version I/O covenant and regression protocol
preview: Define and publish a machine-readable covenant for pinned model versions that guarantees I/O shape, parameter aliases, and a published performance signature. The covenant will be enforced by onboarding checks and the regression pipeline. This ticket scopes the contract format, tolerated deltas, validation harness, and the publishing flow to the model catalog. Goal: give product teams a clear SLA-like promise for pinned models that balances strictness (prevent regressions) with pragmatic adapters (parameter aliasing, safe canonicalizers).

title: Model-tailored KV-slice memory profiles for long-context Llama/Mistral/Qwen variants
preview:
Background:
Redwood's serving runtime benefits from per-model memory profiles that encode KV residency, slab sizes, and eviction heuristics. Existing generic profiles are conservative for long-context variants of Llama/Mistral/Qwen: they either waste GPU headroom or trigger OOMs under realistic long-sequence workloads.

Objective:
Produce model-specific, quant-variant-aware memory profiles ("KV-slice profiles") for Llama, Mistral, and Qwen families that optimize GPU utilization for long-context configs (16k-64k tokens) while preserving latency SLOs and reducing OOM incidence. Profiles should be consumable by the allocator and the routing layer to enable safe fallbacks to lower-footprint variants.

Scope & Non-Goals:
- In scope: offline profiling harness, profile data format, allocator knobs (slab size, span thresholds), KV-slice residency policies, eviction weighting, and integration tests with continuous batching.
- Out of scope: model training changes, model architecture modifications, and non-GPU runtime targets.

Success criteria / acceptance:
- 25–40% reduction in average GPU resident KV footprint for evaluated long-context workloads without increasing 95th percentile generation latency by >5%.
- OOM rate reduction to <0.5% on synthetic and customer-derived long-context traces.
- Auto-generated profiles for at least one quant variant (8-bit) of each model family and documented rollout plan for production traffic.

Proposed approach (experiments):
1) Offline microbench profiler: run representative sequences (2k/8k/32k/64k) across model variants and quantizations to capture per-layer KV page residency, peak allocator occupancy, and fragmentation metrics. Output: CSV + canonical profile JSON. Link: https://confluence.redwood.com/display/INF/kv-slice-profiles-harness

2) KV-slice profile format: small JSON describing per-layer expected residency curve, prefer-slab-size, eviction-weight, and batching-hint (max prefetched tokens). Profiles are intentionally conservative during canary.

3) Dynamic slice sizing heuristic: use model footprint + request sequence length histogram to compute per-GPU slice limits. Heuristic: base_slice = footprint * (seq_len / baseline_len) * safety_factor; safety_factor tuned per-model.

4) Eviction policy tuning: hybrid LRU+weight where layers with heavy KVs (attention keys/values) get lower eviction priority; quant-aware penalties for 8-bit variants. Implement a grace window to avoid thrashing with continuous batching.

5) Allocator improvements: introduce slab compaction triggers when fragmentation > threshold and free headroom < X MB. Validate performance impact (compaction must not spike latency SLOs).

6) Continuous batching integration: add batch-aware residency estimator so batch formation respects slice limits. If batch would exceed slice budget, prioritize earliest requests and optionally fall back to smaller model (via routing policy).

Testing plan:
- Unit tests for profile parser and heuristic calculator.
- Simulated load tests against long-context replay traces (customer A, internal long-doc corpus) on dedicated nodes (p3/onnx setups).
- Canary rollout: start with 5% traffic on runtime-1.22 with telemetry hooks (alloc headroom, OOMs, token throughput, p50/p95 latency). Grafana dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.com/d/kv-profiles-longctx

Risks & mitigations:
- Incorrect profiles cause regressions / OOMs: mitigate with conservative default safety_factor=1.5, trial canary, and automatic rollback if OOM rate > 1%.
- Compaction latency spikes: gate compaction behind latency budget and run on idle cycles where possible.
- Interaction with existing batching heuristics: coordinate with batching owners (see ENG-31472) and run combined stress tests.

Rollout steps:
1) Produce canonical profiles for 8-bit of Llama-2-Chat-70B, Mistral-Chat-22B, Qwen-14B: offline validation + PR (runtime + allocator).
2) Internal canary on dedicated capacity (10% traffic) for two weeks.
3) Expand to 50% with staggered region rollout.
4) Full production enable with opt-out flag for customers.

Artifacts & links:
- Profiler harness: https://github.com/redwood-inference/kv-profile-harness/pull/87
- Design doc: https://confluence.redwood.com/display/INF/Model-Tailored-KV-Slice-Profiles
- Perf results (initial): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3c-kv-profiles-report (internal)

Open questions / decisions needed:
- Should profile generation be part of the model onboarding pipeline (yes/no)? Recommendation: yes for any model accepted into catalog.
- Safety_factor default (1.2 vs 1.5) — PM input required.

Next steps:
- Diego to complete profiler run for the three target models by 2025-03-10.
- Create runtime PR to add profile parser and eviction-weight hook (ENG-915432-rt-run) and assign reviewers from allocator team.
- Schedule combined stress test with batching team (owner: Priya Nair).

title: Rotation scoping mismatch causing privilege escalation between orgs during token refresh
preview: Multiple customers reported unexpected cross-organization access following API key rotations and token refreshes in Dedicated and Private deployments. Investigation shows that under a rare combination of rolling rotations and scaled routing, the token refresh endpoint returned a session token scoped incorrectly (missing tenant binding), allowing a refreshed token to be used against a different org's resources. Hosted deployments are largely unaffected due to a separate auth proxy that enforces org headers; the issue appears in our self-hosted auth-service path used by Dedicated/Private customers. This ticket tracks the immediate remediation, root cause fix, test coverage, rollout, and customer communications.

title: Warm-Standby Certification & Region Handoff Orchestrator (hopscotch method)
preview: Goal: Implement an orchestrator and certification pipeline that exercises multi-region failover semantics and validates warm-standby readiness using a staggered 'hopscotch' priming pattern to avoid synchronized cache churn. This ticket covers: (1) a controller that coordinates region selection and tier routing decisions during outage windows, (2) an automated readiness scoring service that runs periodic probes and computes a single readiness_score per region+tier+model_variant, (3) an acceptance/test harness to certify warm standby pools before they are accepted as active failover targets, and (4) telemetry and alerting to detect degraded priming or variant incompatibilities. Context: previous warm-start efforts experienced synchronous cache invalidations when all standby pools followed the same priming cadence. The hopscotch method staggers priming across regions and model variants so at least one candidate remains cache-hot while others are being refreshed. This reduces client-visible latency spikes during cross-region handoffs and improves success rate of automatic failovers. Scope: orchestrator logic + readiness probe definitions + certificate issuance for standby pools + rollout plan and runbook updates. Out of scope: changes to model training or format conversions; separate ticket will handle model-variant packaging if needed.

title: Synthetic Load Validator and Reservation Autocalibration Playbook
preview:
Summary:
During a capacity incident on 2025-02-10 we observed degraded throughput across several Dedicated clusters. Investigation pointed to a combination of: (1) under-provisioned tenant reservations due to stale traffic estimates, (2) noisy-neighbor workloads that generated burst token exhaustion, and (3) burst policy thresholds that allowed aggressive short-term bursts without adequate backoff, leading to cascading latency spikes.

Goal:
Create a reproducible synthetic load validator and an automated reservation autocalibration playbook that: (a) reproduces the incident class with controlled noisy tenants and realistic KV/cache utilization, (b) derives corrective reservation deltas, and (c) produces safe degraded-burst fallback policies that reduce tail latency without unduly impacting throughput.

Scope and non-goals:
- Scope: Dedicated cluster behavior, tenant reservation sizing, burst token lifecycle, telemetry augmentations, runbook changes, CI gating for reservation changes.
- Non-goals: Changes to customer-visible pricing, on-prem deployment packaging, or host-level kernel changes.

Incident timeline (high level):
- 2025-02-10 02:14 UTC: Alert: sustained 99th pct latency > 2.5s across cluster-dedicated-12.
- 02:18 UTC: Autoscaler attempted to add capacity but reservation rebalancer was delayed by lock contention.
- 02:23 UTC: Several tenants reported 429s when bursts were consumed; tokenbucket metrics show exhaustion event spikes.
- 02:45 UTC: Manual mitigation: soft-fence noisy tenants and temporarily increase reservation of impacted tenants.

Root causes (synthesized):
- Reservation mismatch: historic quota computation used an exponentially weighted moving average tuned for steady-state, underreacting to sudden growth of 2.5x in a top tenant's QPS.
- Burst policy gap: burst allowance (token refill rate) was permissive but lacked graceful decay; no intermediate throttling step between full allowance and hard throttles.
- Telemetry blind spots: synthetic replay tests were not run for cross-tenant KV cache eviction patterns which amplified per-request cost.
- Operational contention: reservation rebalance run serialized under global lock; during incident it queued behind long-running topology reconciliation.

Proposed action items (short, medium, long):
1) Short (this sprint):
  - Build \"synthetic-load-validator\" harness that can emulate: multi-tenant steady + burst traffic, KV cache pressure, variable sequence lengths, and controlled noisy behavior. (Owner: ENG-ops)
  - Add tokenbucket-exhaustion alert with correlated reservation-change events in Grafana and link to runbook. (Owner: Observability)
  - Implement emergency soft-fence API: quick-apply tenant isolation mode that limits new bursts for a tenant while preserving inflight inference. (Owner: runtime)
2) Medium (next 2 sprints):
  - Autocalibration algorithm: forecast-driven reservation adjustment using short-window percentile (P90 over 10m + burst headroom) plus hysteresis and cooldown. Provide dry-run outputs in staging for 24h before primetime. (Owner: scheduler)
  - Replace global rebalance lock with per-pool coordination and deadline-based preemption to avoid queueing. (Owner: allocator)
  - Add synthetic test step to CI that runs a 30m noisy-neighbor scenario and validates no 99th-pt uplift beyond baseline. (Owner: testing infra)
3) Long (Q2+):
  - Policy simulation tooling to model cluster-level fairness and revenue impact of reservation deltas. (Owner: capacity-analytics)
  - Explore tiered burst decay policies (soft-throttle -> token-rate-reduction -> hard-throttle) with configurable knobs per customer tier. (Owner: runtime/policy)

Acceptance criteria:
- Synthetic harness reproduces observed tokenbucket exhaustion within 10% of incident metrics when fed historical traffic profile.
- Autocalibration performs reservation adjustments that reduce 99th-pt latency by >= 30% in the noisy scenario while keeping overall throughput within 5% of baseline.
- Emergency soft-fence can be applied in < 90 seconds and documented in the runbook.
- CI synthetic test runs in under 40 minutes and fails the PR if 99th-pt latency or reservation drift regression occurs.

Design notes and trade-offs:
- Forecasting approach: choose between reactive short-window percentile (less aggressive) vs ML model (more accurate but operationally heavier). We'll begin with deterministic percentile+cooldown for safety, with a supervised ML model as future work.
- Synthetic fidelity: running full-scale GPU-backed synthetic tests is expensive. We'll use a tiered strategy: simulated CPU-only high-level load for quick CI checks, and nightly full-GPU clusters in staging for high-fidelity validation.
- Soft-fence semantics: design to avoid disrupting in-flight requests by throttling new token grants; this reduces blast radius while preserving ongoing responses.

Runbook and CI changes:
- Add step in Dedicated Incident runbook: \"Apply soft-fence to tenant -> verify tokenbucket metrics -> run synthetic validator in local-mode\". Include rollback steps for soft-fence.
- CI: add job `noisy_neighbour_smoke` that executes the lightweight harness and validates P95/P99 and reservation stability.

Telemetry and dashboards to build:
- Grafana: \"Reservation Autocalibrator\" dashboard: per-tenant reservation, estimated desired reservation, tokenbucket fill-rate, exhaustion events, KV-eviction rate.
- Alerting: new composite alert combining tokenbucket_exhaustion OR reservation_delta > 3x within 5m, with pager if customer_impact==high.

Owners and collaborators:
- Runtime: Priya Nair (design soft-fence, burst decay)
- Scheduler/Allocator: Liam Patel (autocalibration, lock removal) [assignee]
- Observability: Marco Ruiz (dashboards, alerts)
- Testing infra: Zoe Kim (synthetic harness + CI jobs)
- Capacity analytics: Elias Novak (policy simulation)

Deliverables and timeline:
- Week 1: lightweight synthetic harness prototype + initial dashboard (MVP).
- Week 2-3: autocalibration prototype in staging (dry-run logs) + lock removal POC.
- Week 4: deploy soft-fence + enable CI synthetic test; measure impact with 2-week canary.

Notes and links:
- Postmortem: https://confluence.redwood.ai/dedicated-capacity-postmortem-2025-02-10
- Incident Grafana: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/alerts/dedicated-capacity-2025-02-10
- Design doc (prototype harness): https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/folders/synthetic-load-validator-proto
- Related tickets: ENG-29315 (spike-mitigation-autotuner), ENG-52489 (reservation policy review), ENG-41322 (burst calibration)

title: Tenant credit-based region routing with variant shadowing and graceful hosted backfill
preview:
Summary:
Design and implement a tenant credit allocator that influences region & tier selection for dedicated capacity while enabling controlled hosted spillback. The allocator issues soft "credits" (soft reservations) based on pre-commit, historical consumption, and SLA tier. Credits drive probabilistic routing decisions: prefer dedicated when credits >= cost_estimate, gradually bleed to hosted when credits are low.

Goals:
- Reduce unexpected hosted spillovers for customers with reserved capacity while allowing controlled, observable backfill to hosted for short bursts.
- Maintain latency SLOs for P0/P1 tenants and keep unit cost predictable for reserved customers.
- Provide a transparent, debuggable fallback mechanism to hosted and to lower-cost model variants.

Design highlights:
1) Credit allocator
- Each tenant has a credit balance updated continuously: credits += reserved_rate * time_window - actual_consumption_cost. Credits are bounded and never drop below a soft negative floor.
- Credit tiers: GREEN (>75%), AMBER (25%-75%), RED (<25%). Routing preference score = base_affinity * f(credit_tier) * tenant_priority_scale.

2) Probabilistic routing with hysteresis
- For each request compute P(dedicated) = sigmoid((score - threshold)/temperature). Add request jitter and short-term moving-average smoothing to avoid flapping.
- Hysteresis windows vary by tier: GREEN (30s), AMBER (2m), RED (10m).

3) Variant shadowing and demotion
- If hosted or lower-cost variant is selected, send a shadow copy to a demoted variant for quality monitoring (no charge to tenant). Shadowing includes: prompt/response hashing, deterministic knob to redact sensitive fields, and TTL for cache.
- Maintain per-variant quality deltas; if demoted variant drops below quality delta threshold for >N requests, automatically escalate to human-in-the-loop review and revert demotion if necessary.

4) Graceful hosted backfill policy
- Backfill is allowed for short bursts if predicted dedicated wait time > backfill_threshold and tenant in RED/AMBER. Backfill must carry metadata (origin:dedicated-backfill, credit_balance, fallback_decision_id) for observability and billing reconciliation.
- Introduce a backfill budget per-tenant per-day to limit cost exposure from hosted spillover.

5) Preemption & recovery
- Dedicated slots are preemptible by a request to reclaim credits: reclaiming triggers a soft-fence preventing re-routing to hosted for a brief cooldown, and emits an event to the tenant alerting them to degraded capacity.
- Recovery path replenishes credits based on committed onboarding schedule and usage smoothing.

Telemetry & observability:
- Emit these metrics at request-level and aggregated: routing_decision(reason), credit_balance_gauge, time_in_tier, backfill_event_count, variant_quality_delta, failed_shadow_rate, cost_delta_per_hour.
- Dashboards: tenant-level SLO heatmap, backfill budget burn-rate, variant-regression alerts.
- Traceable IDs: every decision must surface fallback_decision_id and policy_version in logs to enable postmortems.

Acceptance criteria:
- Simulated 48-hour stress test shows >60% reduction in unexpected hosted spillovers for tenants with >50% reserved commits compared to baseline routing.
- No increase in P0 99th p99 latency beyond 5% during simulated burst tests.

Rollout plan:
1) Canary: enable credit allocator for 2 internal tenants and one low-risk external tenant (1 week).
2) Monitoring: create dashboards and an automated guardrail that disables backfill when budget burn-rate exceeds 120% of expected.
3) Progressive exposure: +10% of tenants per day with automated rollback on regression.
4) Post-rollout: 2-week observation then adjust tier thresholds and hysteresis windows based on real traffic.

Open questions / tradeoffs:
- How to reconcile multi-region commitments where tenant reserves span multiple hemisheres? Proposal: maintain per-region credits with cross-region transfer cap. This adds complexity but preserves locality.
- Determining cost_estimate for long-running streaming requests. We propose a moving-window model that predicts token consumption after N tokens.
- Privacy: shadowing preserves request fingerprints but must redact PII; integrate with KMS and redact rules from private deployments.

text:
Summary:
Design and implement a tenant credit allocator that influences region & tier selection for dedicated capacity while enabling controlled hosted spillback. The allocator issues soft "credits" (soft reservations) based on pre-commit, historical consumption, and SLA tier. Credits drive probabilistic routing decisions: prefer dedicated when credits >= cost_estimate, gradually bleed to hosted when credits are low.

Goals:
- Reduce unexpected hosted spillovers for customers with reserved capacity while allowing controlled, observable backfill to hosted for short bursts.
- Maintain latency SLOs for P0/P1 tenants and keep unit cost predictable for reserved customers.
- Provide a transparent, debuggable fallback mechanism to hosted and to lower-cost model variants.

Design highlights:
1) Credit allocator
- Each tenant has a credit balance updated continuously: credits += reserved_rate * time_window - actual_consumption_cost. Credits are bounded and never drop below a soft negative floor.
- Credit tiers: GREEN (>75%), AMBER (25%-75%), RED (<25%). Routing preference score = base_affinity * f(credit_tier) * tenant_priority_scale.

2) Probabilistic routing with hysteresis
- For each request compute P(dedicated) = sigmoid((score - threshold)/temperature). Add request jitter and short-term moving-average smoothing to avoid flapping.
- Hysteresis windows vary by tier: GREEN (30s), AMBER (2m), RED (10m).

3) Variant shadowing and demotion
- If hosted or lower-cost variant is selected, send a shadow copy to a demoted variant for quality monitoring (no charge to tenant). Shadowing includes: prompt/response hashing, deterministic knob to redact sensitive fields, and TTL for cache.
- Maintain per-variant quality deltas; if demoted variant drops below quality delta threshold for >N requests, automatically escalate to human-in-the-loop review and revert demotion if necessary.

4) Graceful hosted backfill policy
- Backfill is allowed for short bursts if predicted dedicated wait time > backfill_threshold and tenant in RED/AMBER. Backfill must carry metadata (origin:dedicated-backfill, credit_balance, fallback_decision_id) for observability and billing reconciliation.
- Introduce a backfill budget per-tenant per-day to limit cost exposure from hosted spillover.

5) Preemption & recovery
- Dedicated slots are preemptible by a request to reclaim credits: reclaiming triggers a soft-fence preventing re-routing to hosted for a brief cooldown, and emits an event to the tenant alerting them to degraded capacity.
- Recovery path replenishes credits based on committed onboarding schedule and usage smoothing.

Telemetry & observability:
- Emit these metrics at request-level and aggregated: routing_decision(reason), credit_balance_gauge, time_in_tier, backfill_event_count, variant_quality_delta, failed_shadow_rate, cost_delta_per_hour.
- Dashboards: tenant-level SLO heatmap, backfill budget burn-rate, variant-regression alerts.
- Traceable IDs: every decision must surface fallback_decision_id and policy_version in logs to enable postmortems.

Acceptance criteria:
- Simulated 48-hour stress test shows >60% reduction in unexpected hosted spillovers for tenants with >50% reserved commits compared to baseline routing.
- No increase in P0 99th p99 latency beyond 5% during simulated burst tests.
- Integration tests for credit accounting, backfill tagging, and variant shadowing coverage >90%.

Rollout plan:
1) Canary: enable credit allocator for 2 internal tenants and one low-risk external tenant (1 week).
2) Monitoring: create dashboards and an automated guardrail that disables backfill when budget burn-rate exceeds 120% of expected.
3) Progressive exposure: +10% of tenants per day with automated rollback on regression.
4) Post-rollout: 2-week observation then adjust tier thresholds and hysteresis windows based on real traffic.

Open questions / tradeoffs:
- How to reconcile multi-region commitments where tenant reserves span multiple hemisheres? Proposal: maintain per-region credits with cross-region transfer cap. This adds complexity but preserves locality.
- Determining cost_estimate for long-running streaming requests. We propose a moving-window model that predicts token consumption after N tokens.
- Privacy: shadowing preserves request fingerprints but must redact PII; integrate with KMS and redact rules from private deployments.

title: Archiveable quant bundles with immutable hash index and safety pilots

title: Tenant-aware audit data lifecycle and cost-QoS for RBAC v2 events
preview:
Background:
Redwood needs predictable cost and query performance for audit events generated by RBAC v2 and SSO/SAML admin actions. Tenant activity patterns vary widely: some tenants generate millions of admin-audit events per day, others generate only a few. Current pipeline writes all events to a single hot index topology which drives up storage and query costs for heavy tenants and increases noise for investigations.

Goals:
- Ensure high-value, security-relevant audit events (RBAC changes, SSO admin actions, KMS operations) are retained and indexed for fast query for the configured retention window.
- Provide tenant-level QoS that limits ingestion cost for high-volume tenants while guaranteeing retention/availability for tenants with compliance needs.
- Introduce intelligent partitioning and coldstore spill for older data to reduce per-tenant storage costs.
- Surface clear cost guardrails and automatic throttles to prevent noisy tenants from impacting cluster performance.

Scope (what this ticket delivers):
1) Ingestion API change: add event priority field (system|admin|audit|debug) and tenant-cost-profile tag so producers can mark critical RBAC/SSO events. Backfill mapper to enrich existing events based on action_type and actor_role.
2) Dynamic partitioning: route events into per-tenant or tenant-group indices depending on monthly event volume thresholds. Small tenants go to shared partitioned indices; large/high-compliance tenants get dedicated indices with stronger indexing and retention.
3) Hot/cold lifecycle: implement TTL-driven rollover into coldstore with compacted index representation (column-pruned) after 30/90/365 days configurable per-tenant. Coldstore objects use compressed append-only blobs to lower storage cost.
4) Cost QoS controller: quota and burst tokens per tenant with automatic backpressure; admin escalation flow for tenants that need more capacity.
5) Query indexing strategy: selective primary indexing for security fields (actor_id, action, resource, outcome) and optional full-text for tenants that opt-in to richer search.
6) RBAC v2 integration: ensure index access respects tenant-level RBAC; SSO admin events are only visible to users with audit_read permission.
7) Observability and alerts: dashboards for per-tenant ingestion rate, storage spend, index latency, and throttling incidents.
8) Migration plan: phased cutover with canary tenants, backfill job to re-index critical historical events for tenants in dedicated indices.

Design notes:
- Use tenant fingerprint (tenant_id % N) to place small-tenant shards into stable partitions to avoid shard explosion.
- Dedicated indices for high-volume tenants should be autoscalable and pinned to dedicated node pools (Dedicated capacity or private).
- Cost QoS controller runs in the ingest path and emits trace/span with reason code when throttling is applied.
- Retention policies are owner-configurable but constrained by organization-wide max retention in compliance mode.

Acceptance criteria:
- Ingest path accepts and persists event priority and tenant-cost-profile metadata.
- Tenants above volume threshold are routed to dedicated index with correct mapping.
- Coldstore rollover reduces hot index storage for 90-day dataset by >=40% in benchmark test.
- Query SLA: admin/audit queries for recent 7 days return within 200ms P95 for dedicated-index tenants under normal load.
- Throttling events emit audit entries and are visible in console alerts.
- RBAC enforcement validated: tenant_restricted indexes are unreadable by users lacking audit_read.

Rollout plan:
- Week 1: schema change and feature flag behind "tenant-qos-audit". Unit/integration tests.
- Week 2: deploy ingest QoS controller to staging, enable for 5 non-prod tenants.
- Week 3: canary enable for 3 low-risk prod tenants; exercise backfill for last 90 days.
- Week 4: enable for top 10 heavy tenants with manual monitoring and ops on-call.
- Post-rollout: 30-day review and iterate on thresholds and cost models.

Monitoring & metrics to add:
- per_tenant_ingest_rate, per_tenant_storage_cost_estimate, throttles_by_reason, index_rollover_latency, coldstore_spill_rate, backfill_progress

Testing plan:
- Load test with synthetic tenants (1x, 10x, 100x loads) to validate partitioning and throttling behavior.
- Verify RBAC access controls by scripted user roles and SSO flows.
- Cost analysis using billing-sim harness to model monthly spend with and without coldstore.

Migration/cleanup:
- Backfill job runs in low-traffic windows and marks completed segments. Old unified indices flagged read-only after backfill and then compacted for long-term archive retention.

Notes on security_review_required: this change introduces schema and access controls touching RBAC and SSO events — schedule SecReview with compliance and cloud security teams before canary.

title: staged-activation-and-lexicon-sync-pipeline-cleanup
preview: Consolidate and simplify the model asset onboarding path to reduce cold-start tail latency and make weight/shard format handling deterministic. This ticket implements a staged activation flow: probe manifests/metadata, reconcile formats, pre-allocate and coalesce shards when possible, and perform a lightweight lexicon sync (vocab warmup + mapping verification) before full model activation. Replace the current ad-hoc format detectors and multiple tokenizer init paths with a single pluggable lexicon initializer that exposes a stable contract for downstream runtime. Also bump the weight-reading dependency (weight-probe-lib) to the latest patch that includes async checksum validation and streaming headers support.

title: Incident retro & action plan for RBACv2 audit-log regression and SAML session anomalies
preview: Summary: On 2025-02-15 we observed a ~45 minute window where RBAC v2 change events were not consistently emitted to the central audit stream and several SAML session refresh events were dropped by the audit pipeline. Symptoms included missing admin role-change entries in the audit dashboard, delayed session invalidation notices, and a spike in console-facing 403s for recently promoted admin users. This ticket captures the RCA, mitigations applied, follow-up engineering work, and verification plan for restoring audit SLOs and hardening admin controls.

title: KV-prefix: hit-rate sensitivity curve and tail-latency memory tradeoff benchmark
preview: Goal: produce a robust, reproducible benchmark that maps KV/prefix cache hit-rate to p95 token latency and memory footprint across representative production-like workloads. Unlike previous load harnesses that focused on end-to-end throughput at a fixed cache size, this work systematically sweeps cache size, prefix retention policy, and request temporal locality to generate hit-rate sensitivity curves and identify knee points where marginal memory increases stop yielding meaningful latency improvements. The harness must output tokens/sec, p50/p95/p99 (in ms and ms/token), memory at steady-state, eviction churn, and a lightweight regression signal for CI.

title: Signal fencing and delegated ingest proxy for dedicated / on-prem observability
preview:
Problem: Dedicated and on-prem customers run remote collectors and forwarders into Redwood’s observability pipeline. Current designs either (a) place heavy responsibility on the customer-side agent to enforce tenant isolation, or (b) accept mixed signals at the perimeter and rely on downstream filters. Both approaches increase blast radius for misconfiguration, leak risk for cross-tenant telemetry, and operational complexity for private deployments.

Goal: Build a delegated ingest proxy — a lightweight, policy-enforcing ingress layer that performs 'signal fencing': validate and bind incoming traces/metrics/logs to tenant-scoped identities, apply tenant-specific sampling & enrichment, and steer signals into isolated pipeline partitions. The proxy must work in VPC connectors and on-prem collectors, support zero-trust transport (mutual TLS), integrate with KMS for per-tenant encryption keys, and expose metrics and traces for its own SLO monitoring.

Scope and features: 
- Auth & identity binding: accept mTLS/client certs + signed tokens from customer sidecar to assert tenant identity; fall back to signed SAML/JWT where certs aren't available.
- Signal fencing ruleset: enforce allowed sources (IP ranges, cert CNs), mapping rules from incoming attributes to internal tenant IDs, and reject/flag ambiguous requests.
- Delegated sampling & enrichment: apply tenant-configured sampling policies (error-priority presampling, head-based retain-on-error) and enrichment (resource tags normalization, redact PII using configured rules).
- Routing & isolation: pin streams to tenant-specific ingest lanes (logical partitions) and optionally to dedicated Kafka topics/storage buckets for Dedicated customers. Support transparent backpressure and graceful degradation to low-cost backup lanes.
- Observability & transparency: expose per-tenant ingress metrics (ingest_rps, drop_rate, sampling_ratio), internal traces for debugging, and structured audit log entries for each admitted/ rejected batch.
- Data residency & encryption: optional per-tenant KMS key selection, envelope encryption for persisted batches, and retention enforcement in the proxy where required for air-gapped on-prem setups.
- Compatibility: OpenTelemetry protocol support (OTLP/gRPC, OTLP/HTTP), Fluent Forwarder, and a small plugin for legacy syslog-based collectors.

Non-goals: full in-proxy analytics or long-term retention; heavy schema transformations beyond tag normalization. The proxy should be lightweight and deterministic.

Design trade-offs considered: 
- Inline vs async validation: we favor inline validation with small batching to ensure early filtering of bad signals. This adds minimal latency (~2-8ms) but prevents downstream leakage.
- Per-tenant dedicated storage vs logical partitioning: to reduce operational overhead for mid-market customers we'll default to logical lanes; allow dedicated physical partitions as a paid option.
- Rich policy language vs simple RBAC: start with an expressive but bounded rule set (allow/deny, sample-rates, enrichments) and plan a policy UI in Console later.

Acceptance criteria: 
- Proxy can successfully authenticate and bind identity for >95% of test collectors in internal fleet.
- End-to-end latency increase <=10ms p50 vs baseline for OTLP/gRPC small batches.
- No cross-tenant telemetry in blue/green canary runs (proof via fuzz tests and synthetic traffic).
- Audit logs show per-batch tenant mapping and rejection reason when applicable.

Implementation plan: 
1) RFC + API sketch and policy JSON schema (Confluence doc).
2) PoC: Go binary supporting OTLP/gRPC with mTLS auth + simple rules engine.
3) Integrate KMS selection & envelope encryption MVP.
4) Add enrichments + sampling engine and per-tenant metrics.
5) End-to-end tests, chaos tests (certificate rotation, partial network partitions).
6) Gradual rollout: internal -> Dedicated customers (canary) -> Private/VPC customers.

Rollout strategy and safety: canary traffic limited to 1% of Dedicated ingress for first two weeks, automated rollback on elevated drop_rate or >1% increase in latency p95. Enable audit-mode (log-only) behind a feature flag for first Private customers to validate mapping rules without rejecting signals.

Monitoring and SLOs: proxy exposes metrics and traces to Redwood's internal monitoring. SLOs: availability 99.95% for ingress path, p95 latency target <=50ms, error rate <0.5% for authenticated traffic. Dashboards: new per-tenant ingestion dashboard, latency/error heatmap, and a cross-tenant leakage detection dashboard.

Testing & QA: unit tests for rules engine, integration tests with OTLP exporters, synthetic cross-tenant fuzz generator, and live canary harness. Performance test plan includes 100k RPS capacity test in a Dedicated-like environment and verifying resource footprint.

Dependencies: ENG-59901 (observability-proxy-splitter-for-vpc-batching) for shared backlog handling primitives, infra-keys team for KMS provisioning, and Console team for future policy UI.

Deliverables: Go proxy repo with API docs, Confluence design, load test results, canary run report, and a Console integration ticket to surface per-tenant metrics.

Links: Confluence RFC: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/OPS/Signal+Fencing+RFC, GH PoC: https://github.com/redwood-inference/obs-delegated-proxy/pulls/12

title: Route Sentry: route-level observability & alerting for embedding+ranker endpoints
preview:
Goal: Provide route-scoped dashboards, tracing enrichment, token-level cost breakdowns, and automated alerts for embedding + reranking endpoints so product owners can detect quality regressions, latency spikes, and cost anomalies tied to specific routes and model versions.

Scope: instrument runtime and scorer layers to emit route_id, request_tags (caller_id, route_name), embedding_model_version, rerank_model_version, candidate_count, kv_cache_hit, batch_size, vector_index_latency_ms, scorer_latency_ms, tokens_generated, token_cost_usd, compute_gpu_ms. Build dashboards per-route (p50/p95 latency, SLI: successful responses, rerank_precision@10, embedding_distance_drift, cost_per_request, cost_per_1k_tokens) and a cross-route heatmap for sudden delta detection. Implement alert rules for: 1) p95 latency increase > 30% for 15m; 2) cost per request increase > 40% over baseline for 1h; 3) rerank_precision@10 drop > 10% on evaluated prompt set; 4) sudden rise in kv_cache_miss rate > 20%.

Acceptance criteria:
- Traces include route_id and model hashes in 100% of scorer spans.
- Per-route dashboard template available in Console and linked to trace explorer.
- Token-level cost decomposition stored as timeseries and exposed in per-route cost tab.
- Three alert policies wired to PagerDuty and Slack channel #sentry-emb-rank.
- Runbook with diagnostic steps and rollbacks checked into Confluence.

Trade-offs & decisions:
- Sampling: keep 1% full traces for 90 days and store aggregated statistics for all requests. Decision balances disk/TCO vs post-facto debugging needs.
- Granularity: we will not store per-request embeddings for long-term; instead persist summary metrics (mean distance, variance) per route to avoid storage blowup.
- Cost attribution: attribute request cost as embedding_cost + rerank_cost + index_lookup_cost; initial token cost uses dk-token-rate with per-model coefficients until runtime metering is accurate.

Risk & mitigations:
- Increased telemetry volume => throttle spans at client library and rely on aggregated metrics pipeline.
- Alert noise => use warm-up windows, require 3 consecutive evaluation windows before PD.

Open questions:
- Should drift alerts be tied to canary vs production model versions? (Proposed: yes — tag by rollout and include canary flag in spans.)
- Long tail routes with low traffic: what baseline should we use? (Proposed: cohort baseline grouping by request size and candidate_count.)

title: SLO cohort preemptor with GPU fragmentation reduction
preview:
Problem statement: Continuous batching currently favors throughput but can cause unfair latency regressions for bursty tenants and creates GPU memory fragmentation from repeated small KV cache evictions. We need an SLO-aware preemption policy that groups tenants into latency cohorts and performs preemptive slot retirement + opportunistic compaction to reduce fragmentation while preserving throughput and overall GPU utilization.

Goals:
- Keep tenant P95/P99 latency within configured SLO bands during normal and bursty loads.
- Improve memory fragmentation (free slab count and largest contiguous free region) to reduce out-of-memory spikes for large requests.
- Maintain end-to-end throughput within 5% of baseline for steady-state workloads.
- Provide observability and safe rollout via feature flags and canaries.

Non-goals: full KV GC redesign, global tenant rebalancer, or changing model selection routing decisions in this ticket.

title: Deprecation sweep: compat helpers, codemods, and automated changelog for SDK v1->v2
preview: Goal: execute a coordinated deprecation sweep across Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs to make v2 the default, provide engineering-safe compatibility helpers and codemods, and ship an automated changelog pipeline so customers can find breaking changes and migration examples. Scope: (1) identify all v1 surface area to be removed/alias'd; (2) implement light-weight runtime shims and opt-in compatibility packages where removal would cause large customer impact; (3) author codemods for common patterns (streaming, retries, structured output -> function-calling); (4) update docs and sample apps with side-by-side v1->v2 examples; (5) add CI migration tests and a changelog automation job that extracts API-level deprecations from source and PR titles. Deliverables: - compat-helpers packages (python redwood_compat, ts @redwood/compat, go compat module) - codemod scripts + examples (jscodeshift for TS, rope/codemod patterns for Python, go/repair tools) - migration page with runnable snippets and diffable examples covering streaming ergonomics, retry semantics, and new structured output patterns - changelog automation that parses PR labels and deprecation annotations and publishes a machine and human-readable changelog artifact (CHANGELOG_API.json + CHANGELOG.md) - rollout plan for toggling v2-as-default in hosted API and deprecation notices for Dedicated/Private nodes Risks & trade-offs: - Heavy-handed shims increase maintenance burden; we prefer opt-in compat packages rather than leaving ambiguous runtime behavior. - Codemods cannot guarantee semantics with custom user wrappers around SDKs; emphasize CLA/backup in migration docs. - Streaming retry semantics: v2's streaming backpressure model is stricter; add an examples section showing how to migrate streaming consumers to the new async iterator + reconnect pattern. Out-of-scope for this ticket: large refactors to internals (kernel selection), language-specific re-architecting of SDKs beyond surface shims. Links and artifacts: - Migration notebook (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SDK/SDK+v1->v2+Deprecation+Sweep - Draft migration page (Drive): https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/folders/SDK-migration-drafts - Draft PRs: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-python/pull/834, https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-js/pull/1201, https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-go/pull/279 - Codemod repo: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-codemods - Changelog automation PoC: https://github.com/redwood-inference/changelog-bot (contains rule set for deprecation parsing and sample webhook) Acceptance criteria: 1) Compat packages published to PyPI/NPM/go proxy with basic test coverage and a README migration example for streaming and retry cases. 2) Codemods able to transform 80% of internal migration patterns used in our sample apps. 3) Changelog automation added to CI that emits CHANGELOG_API.json and a human CHANGELOG.md on release branches. 4) Docs updated with step-by-step migration recipes and a rollback section. 5) Runbook for customer support and SEs describing common migration gotchas and recommended fixes.

title: LLM handoff curation and profile publish orchestrator
preview:
Goal: Implement an automated, auditable pipeline that takes community weights -> deterministic conversion -> multi-variant runtime artifacts -> smoke/quality/latency validation -> generates a canonical pinned version and publishes a standardized performance profile. This ticket covers orchestration and policy decisions for curation (safety heuristics, trusted manifests), deterministic conversion reproducibility, multi-quantization candidate generation, and the profile publish flow that feeds Console and the model catalog.

Scope: 1) Intake manifest validation (signed/unsigned handling, provenance metadata extraction), 2) deterministic conversion engine invocation and artifact hashing, 3) compatibility matrix enforcement (opset/kernel constraints), 4) candidate quantization passes (FP16, q4_0, q4_k_maybe) with smoke latency/accuracy checks, 5) end-to-end KV/cache warmup smoke harness, 6) profile aggregation and publish to model-catalog, 7) automatic pin creation and rollout gating into staging.

Design constraints / decisions: - Use convex-convert v0.9+ as conversion baseline for reproducibility (deterministic build flags enabled). - Keep original weight blobs in immutable storage with signed manifest; converted artifacts live in artifact-store under canonical pin. - For quantization we run three deterministic passes and pick the best candidate that meets quality SLOs (top-1/EM on small eval shards + no more than 10% latency regression vs FP16 baseline). - Security gate: models flagged by heuristic safety scanner (safety-scan-v2) land in a quarantine bucket and require manual review. - Publish profile fields: model_size, peak_memory_32seq, median_tok_latency_8seq, throughput_128batch, quality_shard_1, quantization_candidates, conversion_artifact_hash, provenance_link.

Acceptance criteria: - Pipeline processes a sample OSS model (weights + repo) end-to-end and produces a canonical pin with published profile JSON that Console can ingest. - Conversion must be reproducible across two runs (artifact hashes match). - Telemetry emitted for every step with success/failure codes and per-step latency. - Security gating and quarantine flow exercised in integration tests.

Out of scope: manual model governance UI, advanced eval suites beyond the smoke shard, long-term retention policy changes.

title: Incident slate: lightweight incident artifact and escalation attachments
preview:
Background:
Redwood's escalation channels (PagerDuty + Slack) currently post full evidence bundles which are heavyweight and often duplicate information across attachments. This work introduces an \"incident slate\": a compact, structured artifact (JSON blob + small rich attachment) generated at signal ingestion and included in PD and Slack notifications. The slate is designed to: surface the minimal set of decision-making facts, link to the detailed evidence bundle, and seed runbook templates and postmortem drafts automatically.

Goals:
- Reduce cognitive load for oncall by surfacing the top 3 signals (latency spike, error class, top affected customers) and immediate remediation hints.
- Ensure the slate can be consumed programmatically by runbook automation and postmortem seeding systems.
- Preserve privacy: redact PII and adhere to retention policies before any external notification.
- Make the slate resilient to rate-limited backends and graceful in cases of missing telemetry.

Success metrics (first 12 weeks):
- Median time-to-triage reduced by 25% for P1 latency incidents across runtime services.
- Oncall positive feedback score >= 4.0 in internal survey for new PD/Slack attachments.
- Automatic postmortem seed acceptance rate >= 70% (seeded drafts require < 2 edits on average).

Design decisions & trade-offs:
1) Compact vs verbose: We selected a 10-field maximum slate instead of full evidence to keep PD cards scannable. Trade-off: may omit low-signal spans that are useful in rare cases; detailed evidence bundle remains linked.
2) Data retention & redaction: Slate will include hashed identifiers (sha256) for customer IDs and redact raw request payloads. This reduces debugging fidelity but is required for privacy and regulator alignment.
3) Generation point: create slate at the tracing ingestion worker (closest to the signal) to minimize telemetry enrichment latency. This increases worker CPU but reduces notification latency.
4) Fallback behavior: If span extraction fails, attach a short summary: \"Trace metadata unavailable — evidence bundle queued\". Prevents noisy PD alerts caused by incomplete payloads.

Slate schema (summary):
- incident_id, short_summary, severity, top_error_class, top_latency_p95_ms, top_3_spans (span_name + relative_time_ms), affected_customers_hashed, suggested_runbook_id, links {evidence_bundle, grafana_slice, trace_link}, generated_at_iso

Acceptance criteria:
- PD integration: PD webhook receives slate JSON in the \"details\" payload and renders a compact card matching the design spec.
- Slack integration: Slack attachment uses Block Kit to show the short_summary + top_error_class and a button linking to the full evidence bundle.
- Runbook seeding: When suggested_runbook_id is present, runbook seeder creates a draft with the slate fields filled and assigns the draft to the incident owner.
- Telemetry: Emit metrics: slate.generated.count, slate.redactions.count, slate.fallback.count, slate.processing.latency_ms (p95/p99).
- Tests: Unit tests for schema validation, e2e test that simulates a latency spike and verifies slate in PD and Slack.

Implementation plan (high level):
1) Slate generator service (4 pts): implement extractor that composes slate from trace + metrics + alerts. Add config to cap included spans to 3 and perform PII redaction.
2) PD adapter (1 pt): extend PD webhook forwarder to embed slate in notification payload and add JSON schema validation.
3) Slack adapter (1 pt): Block Kit template and link handling + localized fallback for mobile clients.
4) Runbook seeder hook (1 pt): small service to accept slate and instantiate runbook draft.
5) Metrics & dashboards (1 pt): new Grafana panels and Alert rules for slate errors/latency.

Rollout plan:
- Week 1 (canary): Internal infra teams only; PD group alerts routed to a dev PD escalation policy; collect feedback.
- Week 2 (expanded): Add a subset of customer-facing services (search, embeddings) behind feature flag.
- Week 3 (full): Default for all new P1 incidents; old attachments deprecated but available via link.
- Post rollout: Measure triage time and iterate on slate fields based on oncall feedback.

QA & validation:
- e2e harness (github action): synthetic load that triggers a latency SLO breach and asserts slate appears in PD and Slack with correct fields.
- Manual walkthrough checklist for SRE oncall: verify redaction, verify runbook seed creation, sanity-check links.

Risks & mitigations:
- Over-redaction reduces debuggability: keep a privacy-exempt audit mode only accessible to SREs for 7 days.
- Increased generation latency: move heavy enrichment to async background job if 95th percentile exceeds 150ms.
- PD rate limits: throttle notifications and collapse repeated slates into a single update with incrementing summary.

Longer-term extensions:
- Integrate with postmortem auto-population: map slate fields to postmortem sections (impact, timeline seeds).
- Enrich slate with model inference traces for Redwood Dedicated/private customers when available.
- Allow customer opt-in for non-redacted slates when contractually permitted.

title: Reduce workspace churn and improve tiling heuristics for FA3 integration
preview: Integrate FlashAttention v3 (FA3) into the serving runtime while eliminating excessive workspace allocation churn and improving tiling decisions for common sequence-length distributions. Current FA3 integration yields higher end-to-end p99 latency on some models due to repeated cudaMalloc/cudaFree cycles and suboptimal tile selection for mixed-length traffic. This ticket scopes an audit, targeted engine changes (workspace reuse, allocator pooling), and new tiling heuristics driven by model profiles.
